entry.title: SAP Joule for Consultants at SAP SAPPHIRE - Orlando 2026
entry.content:
At SAP, we build on a strong track record of innovation. With SAP Joule for Consultants, we extend the power of generative AI across our consultant ecosystem, empowering professionals to work more productively.

While our initial focus has been on leveraging the SAP Knowledge Base to support your needs, we also recognize the importance of integrating custom-specific knowledge. In this context, we’re excited to share our latest updates and announcements.

At SAPPHIRE – Orlando 2026, you can meet our experts at sessions

  • FOU2662 - Accelerating Migration and Transformation with SAP Joule for Consultants (5/11 4-4:20pm EDT Theater 18)
  • FOU2663 - How SAP Joule for Consultants Helps Partners Deliver Faster (5/13 2:30-2:50pm EDT Theater 12)

Demo Pods 158 to learn the following:

  • Prompting best practices
  • Accelerating migration and transformation
  • New features and innovation
  • Product vision and evolution
  • Activation process for SAP Joule for Consultants

If you are just about to get started, you can find the quick activation resources below:

More official resources:

  • Help Page – SAP Joule for Consultants
  • SAP Discovery Center Mission – Enable SAP Joule for Consultants
  • SAP Community Blog Post - Getting Started with Joule for Consultants - Setup Guide
  • What’s New – Latest released features (filter with Product – SAP Joule for Consultants)

entry.title: SAP Joule- The "Totally not an official ITSM connector"
entry.content:
SAP Joule isn’t a magical ITSM plug that sits between systems wearing a badge. It’s more like an enthusiastic translator at a networking event who says, “Oh, you’ve got an incident in ServiceNow? I’ll just whisper that into the API for you.”

So instead of “SAP Joule is an ITSM connector” Think: “SAP Joule intelligent assistant is that clever middle layer in SAP applications like S4HANA, Build Work Zone/Fiori that chats with ServiceNow via APIs, turns your casual complaints into structured incidents, and then politely updates them when new test results arrive—like a very overqualified messenger who never complains about copy-pasting JSON and knows how to get the job done through APIs while you just stand there wondering how it became so competent..”

In short: it’s not officially an ITSM connector , but it fakes it quite efficiently, powered entirely by APIs and good intentions. ( Using SAP Joule Studio in Build and custom Joule skills)

Incident creation from SAP Joule

When users interact with SAP Build Work Zone/Fiori or any SAP Joule enabled application, SAP Joule can act as an embedded AI assistant that interprets user requests, system alerts, or test outcomes. Instead of switching tools, users can simply describe an issue in natural language (for example, “login failure in payroll app after deployment or system dump”), and SAP Joule can:

 

  • Format the problem into a structured ITSM ticket
  • Create a new incident directly in ServiceNow

 

This reduces friction between business users and IT operations teams, ensuring incidents are logged consistently and quickly and with improved quality.

As shown below, SAP Joule asks to provide some mandatory inputs ( can be customized), which can be ingested in one single well drafted prompt, separated by commas and an incident is submitted in service now in Realtime.

Incident successfully created with the right inputs. Some inputs can be made mandatory to control the incident quality and later operational analysis.

Updating incidents with testing results

Beyond creation, SAP Joule can also manage the lifecycle of incidents. As users validate fixes or reproduce issues, they can feed results back into SAP Joule, which then:

  • Updates existing ServiceNow incidents with comments
  • Reflects status changes such as “In Testing,” “Resolved,” or “Reopened” (possible)

For example, after testing, a user can simply instruct SAP Joule to append results to an open incident. The update is immediately reflected in ServiceNow, ensuring real-time synchronization between SAP and ITSM systems.

Advantages of using SAP Joule for incident generation

Integrating SAP Joule with ServiceNow could provide several operational benefits:

  • Faster incident creation: Users can log incidents directly from business workflows without switching platforms
  • Reduced manual effort: Natural language processing eliminates the need for structured form filling
  • Improved accuracy: Context-aware AI reduces incomplete or inconsistent incident descriptions.
  • Seamless integration: Direct connection between SAP applications and ServiceNow improves end-to-end visibility
  • Real-time updates: Test results and comments can be pushed instantly into active incident

Summary

I only discussed two possibilities but the capabilities could go far beyond this.

  • Search incidents or Search known solutions
  • Read knowledge articles in service now (Document grounding with Service Now)
  • Auto assign Category/sub-category based on inputs or past incidents.

By positioning SAP Joule as an intelligent ITSM companion, organizations could bridge SAP-centric business processes with ServiceNow-based incident management. SAP Joule Embedded within SAP applications, could transform how incidents are created, enriched, and resolved—making IT operations more responsive, automated, and closely aligned with business activity.

Note:  GenAI was used for language refinement and tone improvement.

entry.title: SAP Joule in Action: Redefining Business Intelligence
entry.content:
Introduction

The evolution of enterprise systems is entering a new phase one where AI is intergarted and actively  collaborates with users. With the introduction of SAP Joule, SAP has positioned itself at the forefront of this transformation by delivering a generative AI copilot across business applications.

SAP Joule AI represents a paradigm shift in how users interact with SAP systems such as SAP S/4HANA, SAP SuccessFactors.

working on AI transformations, I have observed a clear shift: business users are no longer satisfied with transactional systems they expect intelligent assistants embedded directly into workflows. Joule aligns exactly with this expectation, bridging the gap between business intent and system execution.

What is SAP Joule?

SAP Joule is a generative AI copilot and more embedded across SAP’s cloud portfolio. It enables users to:

  • Interact with SAP systems using natural language
  • Automate complex workflows
  • Generate insights in real time
  • Enhance decision-making with contextual intelligence

 

Built on the SAP Business Technology Platform, Joule leverages AI models integrated with enterprise data while ensuring governance, security, and compliance.

From my implementation experience, one of the biggest challenge in SAP systems has been user navigation complexity. Joule’s conversational interface eliminates the need for deep transaction code knowledge, which is particularly valuable in large-scale deployments involving non-technical users.

Why SAP Joule Matters

Traditional ERP systems are transactional. SAP Joule makes them conversational and intelligent.

 

Key Value Drivers:

  • Productivity Boost: Reduce manual effort through AI-assisted automation
  • User Experience Transformation: Shift from UI-driven to intent-driven interactions
  • Faster Decision-Making: Real-time contextual insights
  • Process Optimization: Intelligent recommendations embedded in workflows

During SAP S/4HANA transformation programs, especially in success factor / supply chain environments, decision-making often depends on multiple reports and manual analysis. Joule has the potential to compress decision cycles from hours to minutes by generating real-time insights directly within the process

SAP Joule Architecture Overview

 

 

A typical Joule-enabled landscape includes:

Core ERP Layer

  • SAP S/4HANA (Digital Core)

AI & Extension Layer

  • SAP BTP (AI services, integration, extensions)

Data Layer

  • SAP Datasphere / SAP HANA Cloud

AI Copilot Layer

  • SAP Joule embedded in Fiori applications

Integration Layer

  • APIs, OData services, Event Mesh

 In S/4HANA  implementations , maintaining a clean core is critical. Joule fits well into this paradigm because it leverages BTP for AI processing, avoiding heavy customizations in the core ERP system.

Implementation Approach for SAP Joule

Phase 1: Readiness Assessment

  • Evaluate system landscape (ECC vs S/4HANA)
  • Identify high-value AI use cases
  • Assess data quality and governance maturity

 

Data readiness is the single biggest success factor. Without harmonized master data and clean transactional data, AI outputs , no matter how advanced , lose business relevance.

Phase 2: Platform Enablement

  • Activate SAP BTP services
  • Configure AI capabilities
  • Ensure connectivity with S/4HANA

Architectural side treat BTP not just as a platform, but as an AI innovation layer. Joule implementation makes it central to the architecture.

Phase 3: Use Case Identification

Common SAP Joule use cases:

Supply Chain

  • Demand forecasting insights
  • Exception handling automation

Procurement

  • Smart supplier recommendations
  • Contract summarization

HR

  • Job description generation
  • Leave insights

 

I see immediate value in navigation  where Joule can proactively suggest actions instead of users searching to find the panel.

Phase 4: Integration & Extension

  • Extend Joule capabilities using SAP BTP
  • Integrate with external AI models if required
  • Enable APIs for custom workflows

In complex enterprise landscapes (e.g., SAP + external logistics platforms), Joule can act as a unified intelligence layer, reducing dependency on multiple dashboards and tools.

Phase 5: Security & Governance

  • Role-based access control
  • Data privacy compliance
  • AI explainability and auditability

From an enterprise architecture standpoint, I see AI governance is not optional. Organizations must define:

  • What data Joule can access
  • How decisions are audited
  • How AI outputs are validated

This has become especially critical in regulated industries.

Phase 6: Change Management

  • User training for conversational AI
  • Adoption strategy
  • Continuous feedback loop

One underestimated challenge is user trust in AI. Adoption improves significantly when users understand:

  • How Joule generates responses
  • When to trust vs validate outputs

Best Practices for SAP Joule Implementation

  • Start with Business Value, Not Technology
  • Prioritize Clean Core in S/4HANA
  • Leverage Standard SAP AI Capabilities First
  • Ensure High Data Quality
  • Adopt Agile AI Deployment Approach

We Started with assistive AI (recommendations) before moving to autonomous AI (decision-making). This phased approach reduces risk and improves adoption.

Challenges to Consider

  • Data readiness and harmonization
  • User adoption resistance
  • AI governance and compliance
  • Integration complexity in hybrid landscapes

In real projects, the biggest blocker is not technology it’s organizational readiness. AI transformation requires alignment across IT, business, and leadership.

Future Outlook

SAP Joule will evolve into a central AI orchestrator, enabling:

  • Autonomous business processes
  • AI-driven decision intelligence
  • Multi-agent enterprise ecosystems

Given my work in Agentic AI, I see Joule as a foundation for multi-agent enterprise systems, where different AI agents collaborate across supply chain, finance, and operations.

Conclusion

SAP Joule represents a shift from transactional ERP systems to intelligent, AI-driven enterprises.

For SAP architects and leaders, the focus should be on aligning AI capabilities with business outcomes, ensuring governance, and building scalable AI-driven ecosystems.

From an SAP architect’s strategic perspective, Joule is not just another feature it is a strategic capability that will redefine how users interact with enterprise systems. Organizations that successfully adopt it will gain a significant competitive advantage in automation, decision-making, and operational efficiency.

entry.title: SAP Joule in Asset Management: Embedded AI Capabilities in S/4HANA 2025
entry.content:
Overview

With S/4HANA 2025 Private Edition, SAP embeds generative AI into Asset Management through SAP Joule, enabling natural‑language interaction across standard SAP Fiori applications. Joule allows maintenance engineers, planners and technicians to retrieve information and execute transactions conversationally while preserving governance and process integrity.

This blog highlights key embedded Joule capabilities for Asset Management, illustrated with real example prompts and screenshots from S/4HANA 2025.

1.      Display Maintenance Orders and Notifications

Joule allows maintenance users to quickly access execution data using natural language prompts.

1.1 Display Maintenance Notifications

Users can retrieve maintenance notifications by specifying equipment or technical objects.

Example prompt

  • Show maintenance notifications of technical object 210100091

SAP Joule displaying maintenance notifications using a natural‑language prompt in Find Maintenance Notification (F2071).

1.2 Display Maintenance Orders

Maintenance orders can be retrieved in a similar way.

Example prompt

  • Show maintenance orders of technical object 210100004

Maintenance orders retrieved via SAP Joule and displayed in Find Maintenance Orders (F2175).

2.      Perform Maintenance Jobs

One of the strongest Joule capabilities is support for maintenance execution, integrated with
F5104A – Perform Maintenance Jobs.

2.1 View Maintenance Jobs

Users can retrieve job lists based on ownership, dates, work centre, or maintenance order.

Example prompt

  • Show my jobs of this week

Maintenance jobs displayed using SAP Joule in Perform Maintenance Jobs (F5104A).

2.2 View Jobs in Calendar

Jobs can also be visualised in a calendar view.

Example prompt

  • Display my jobs in calendar

Calendar view of maintenance jobs generated via SAP Joule in Perform Maintenance Jobs (F5104A).

Example prompt

  • Display all maintenance job having technical object 210100091 in calendar

Display maintenance jobs for a specific technical object in calendar.

2.3 Assign Jobs and Change Status

Technicians can assign jobs and update status conversationally.

Example prompts

  • Assign job of order 4001701 and operation 0010 to me
  • Start the job of order 4001701 and operation 0010

Assigning and updating maintenance jobs using SAP Joule in Perform Maintenance Jobs (F5104A).

2.4 Record Time on Jobs

Time confirmations can also be entered using natural language.

Example prompt

  • Record 1 hour for job of order 4001701 and operation 0010

Recording actual work time on a maintenance job using SAP Joule in SAP S/4HANA Asset Management.

2.5 View Materials and Services

Material and service visibility is available in calendar context.

Example prompt

  • Show materials for my jobs in a calendar

Material requirements for maintenance jobs displayed using SAP Joule.

Example prompt

  • Show services for maintenance jobs assigned to me in calendar

Service requirements for maintenance jobs displayed using SAP Joule.

3.      Create and Manage Maintenance Events

Joule also supports maintenance event planning, tightly integrated with planning buckets.

3.1 Create Maintenance Events

Users can create maintenance events using natural language.

Example prompt

  • Create a maintenance event for Compressor of type BI from 01/06/2026 at 9:00:00 until 15/06/2026 at17:00:00

Creating a maintenance event using SAP Joule with automatic planning bucket creation in S/4HANA 2025.

3.2 View Upcoming Maintenance Events

Upcoming events can be displayed with or without time filters.

Example prompt

  • Show upcoming maintenance events

Upcoming maintenance events displayed via SAP Joule with access to planning buckets.

3.3 View Maintenance Orders assigned to a Maintenance Event

Maintenance Orders assigned to a maintenance event can be displayed.

Example prompt

  • Show maintenance orders and notifications that are assigned to a maintenance event 00000001

Displaying maintenance orders assigned to a Maintenance Event.

 

3.4 Assign Orders to Maintenance Events

Maintenance orders can be assigned to events conversationally.

Example prompt

  • Show maintenance orders that can be assigned to maintenance event 00000001 --> Open Order

Viewing maintenance order details before assigning to a Maintenance Event.

Example prompt

  • Show maintenance orders that can be assigned to maintenance event 00000001 --> Add to Event

Assigning a maintenance order to a maintenance event using SAP Joule.

Example prompt

  • Assign order 4001721 to maintenance event 00000001

Assigning a specific maintenance order to a Maintenance Event.

Key Takeaways

  • SAP Joule brings conversational AI into transactional Asset Management.
  • Users can retrieve data, execute jobs, record time, and manage events using natural language.
  • Tight integration with SAP Fiori apps ensures governance and consistency.
  • S/4HANA 2025 marks a significant step towards AI‑assisted maintenance execution and planning.

References

  • SAP Help
  •  Maintenance Event Planning Agent

entry.title: SAP AI: Beyond the Hype and Into the Real World
entry.content:
Three days in Excel hell. Twenty supplier bids. One AI agent that knows your data. This is the SAP AI story your vendor deck won't tell you — first in a series.

I've been on the digital transformation journey for more years than I care to admit. I truly believe that constant evolution is the only way to stay relevant in procurement, but lately, it feels like the word "transformation" has been completely hijacked by a single two-letter acronym: AI. Everywhere I turn, AI is the only topic in the room. It is being pitched as the ultimate cure-all, yet for most of us, the daily reality still involves wrestling with legacy systems and messy data.

After endless conversations about AI's impact today, where it is going, and how it fits into our daily lives, I've realized that what we need is not more noise, but a clear roadmap.

My intention with this series is to provide a solid understanding of where we are today, set some realistic expectations, and point out where the magic really happens when we get it right.

We will look at what SAP is doing broadly with Business AI, while zooming in specifically on the procurement space where the impact is most immediate. Each post in this series will focus on a specific piece of the puzzle or a real-world scenario, helping you not only navigate this AI transformation but ensuring you actually see tangible value at the end of it. And I promise: no marketing fluff and no buzzword bingo.

 

The Anatomy of Success: How the Pieces Actually Fit Together

Stop thinking about AI as a single feature you can switch on. It's a high-performance system built from three distinct, non-negotiable layers. Pull any one out and the whole thing fails to deliver value. Here's the blueprint:

 The three layers of the SAP AI stack. Remove any one and the system fails to deliver value.

Each layer has a clear job. BTP is where your code actually runs and your security lives. SAP Business Data Cloud is the part that prevents your AI from getting confused — it makes sure a "Supplier" in one system is the same thing as a "Vendor" in another. Joule is the conversational front door that takes your natural-language request and orchestrates the work across every back-end system. Everything else in this post depends on these three working together.

 

Four Flavors of SAP AI You'll Actually Encounter

Not all AI is created equal — and you'll meet at least four different flavors in any real SAP deployment. Each one solves a different problem and shows up in your workflow in a different way. Here's how to recognize them:

 Four flavors of SAP AI. Each one solves a different problem — and you'll meet all of them

 

Talk Is Cheap. Let's Look at a Real Scenario.

We've all heard the lofty promises of AI, but talk is notoriously cheap. Instead of another high-level theory, I want to show you a practical scenario that has already been built and is currently saving procurement experts hundreds of hours. This is not some futuristic concept waiting for a release date — it's how we're solving real-world complexity right now.

 The Bid Analysis Agent in action — what 80% of the heavy lifting looks like.

WHAT HAPPENS TODAY WITHOUT AI

You're stuck in Excel hell for three days. You're manually pivoting data, trying to run "what if" scenarios, and likely making a few coffee-fueled typos along the way. By the time you finally have an answer, the market data has already shifted and your analysis is effectively a historical document.

THE BASIC AI EXPERIENCE

You try to use a generic, public AI tool. You upload a spreadsheet and ask for an analysis. It gives you a shallow "cheapest is best" answer, hallucinates a few shipping routes that don't exist, and potentially leaks your sensitive pricing data to the public web. Fast, but dangerous — and completely lacking business context.

THE REALISTIC SAP AI FUTURE

You use a Bid Analysis Agent grounded in your actual Ariba data. You ask Joule to show the award scenario that minimizes risk if fuel prices spike. The agent evaluates the trade-offs, highlights bid outliers, and suggests a data-driven strategy in seconds by factoring in unit prices, shipping, and risk data. You're still the decision-maker — but the AI has handled 80% of the analytical heavy lifting for you.


Straight Talk: Paying the Data Tax

AI in 2026 is not about replacing the procurement professional. It is about capacity. It's about getting that 20% to 30% of your week back from repetitive, manual work.

But let's be real about the Data Tax. I'm not suggesting every organization is starting from scratch. In fact, most customers I talk to have done a great job with their supplier and master data. The real mess usually hides elsewhere: in the offline spreadsheets, the unstructured sourcing history, and the fragmented procurement processes that haven't quite made it into the system yet.

 

Two data realities. One of them turns your AI into a confident liar.

Good data is structured, centralized, and consistent. It means having one version of the truth, so that when you ask an AI for a risk assessment, it isn't hallucinating because it found three different versions of the same contract.

Bad data is the fragmented manual sprawl — the tribal knowledge and the manual workarounds that live outside your digital core.

When you try to use AI with bad data, the implications are simple: the AI becomes "confidently wrong." It will give you an answer, but that answer will be based on a fragment of reality. Paying the Data Tax is not a one-time activity or a weekend cleanup. It's an ongoing evolution in how you store and leverage your information — a commitment to continuous improvement where your AI outputs steadily improve as your data strategy matures.

You simply cannot ignore the data layer of the architecture and expect the interaction layer to give you accurate results.

What's Next

Moving forward, this series will dive deep into the specific AI scenarios and features that drive actual savings in both time and money. I'll be sharing not just what we're building at SAP, but also the real-world best practices I'm seeing from other leading organizations that are successfully closing the "execution gap." There's a wealth of peer knowledge out there that can benefit us all.

I have specific use cases planned, but I'm always interested in your perspective. If there's a particular challenge you're facing or a piece of the puzzle you want to see solved, let me know what you'd like me to cover in future chapters.

entry.title: Why Software Today Feels Like the Auto Industry Before Tesla
entry.content:
In my role as a Platform Product Manager at SAP, I see every day how the software industry is entering a phase that strongly resembles the moment when the automotive industry shifted from combustion engines to electric and software-defined vehicles. On the surface, we see incremental improvements. In reality, a fundamental platform shift is happening...

....and it is leading us towards something even bigger: The Autonomous Enterprise.

What the “skateboard” platform and software-defined vehicle were for the auto industry is, in many ways, what AI-first, platform-centric architectures are becoming for enterprise software. At SAP, this is shaping our business and platform strategy. From Business AI and Joule to the SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) as the backbone of intelligent, end-to-end business processes and, ultimately, more autonomous business operations.

From Feature Software to AI-First Business Platforms

In the automotive industry, the real game changer was not just the battery. It was the completely rethought vehicle platform: centralized computing, over-the-air updates, and the idea that value creation truly starts after the car leaves the factory.

Enterprise software is undergoing a similar transition. We are moving from isolated feature development in individual applications to building platforms where data, processes and AI are tightly integrated. SAP BTP plays a central role as the data, integration and AI layer across core processes such as finance, supply chain and HR. AI is no longer “just another module”, but an integral part of how we design the platform: from embedded Business AI and Joule to AI agents that orchestrate end-to-end business scenarios and automate decisions.

Organizations that simply “bolt on” an AI feature to existing products are effectively doing what some OEMs did when they tried to electrify a combustion chassis: it works at first, but it does not scale and it does not fully exploit the potential of a new platform.

From Software-Defined Vehicles to the Autonomous Enterprise

If electrification and software-defined vehicles were the first big step, autonomous driving is the next logical stage in the automotive transformation. It builds on the same platform foundation - sensors, connectivity, centralized compute - but moves the value proposition from “assisted driver” to “self-driving system”.

The same pattern is now emerging in the enterprise:

  • AI first made individual processes “assisted”, through recommendations, anomaly detection, and copilots embedded in workflows.

  • The next step is autonomous processes that can sense, decide and act across systems: think of an order that triggers fulfillment, adjusts capacity, renegotiates freight and updates the forecast without human intervention. With humans supervising and steering, not micro-managing.

  • The long-term vision is an autonomous enterprise: a company that continuously optimizes itself based on real-time data, policies and goals, orchestrated by AI and agents running on a robust platform.

Just as autonomous driving requires a software-defined vehicle as its base, the autonomous enterprise requires a software-defined business: clean data, consistent process models and a platform that can host intelligent agents and automation at scale.

The Shift in Value Creation – From Hardware to Services, From Apps to Platforms, From Automation to Autonomy

In the automotive world, value creation is shifting from one-time hardware sales to software functions, data-driven services and mobility ecosystems.

We are seeing a similar pattern in enterprise software, with an additional twist:

  • From license-centric thinking to cloud subscriptions and platform consumption.

  • From isolated IT projects to AI-enabled, end-to-end business processes on a unified platform.

  • From task-level automation to goal-driven autonomy, where systems can propose and execute actions in line with business objectives and guardrails.

Here, SAP has a structural advantage: we are deeply embedded in mission-critical business processes around the globe and have access to highly relevant, trusted business data. Exactly the context that enterprise AI and autonomous scenarios need to be meaningful rather than experimental.

Platform Thinking from a Platform Product Manager’s Perspective

As a Platform Product Manager, I work right at this intersection. We no longer look at integration, APIs, events, data and AI services as a loose toolbox, but as a coherent business platform on which customers and partners can build their own innovation....and progressively more autonomous capabilities.

In practice, this means:

  • Designing and Evolving SAP BTP as an open, enterprise-grade foundation for AI and agentic use cases, not a closed island. Agents need a consistent way to access data, call business APIs and trigger workflows across systems.

  • Treating integration and data quality as the backbone : without a clean integration and data layer, AI remains a demo and autonomy remains a slideware vision.

  • Creating a developer and platform experience where APIs, events, AI services, rules and extensibility tell a single story: sensing events, reasoning over data and acting through workflows and business objects.

In other words: if software-defined vehicles needed a “central compute brain”, the autonomous enterprise needs a business platform brain – and that is where the platform strategy becomes truly differentiating.

To make this more tangible, consider the Order‑to‑Cash (O2C) process as the enterprise equivalent of the path from driver assistance to autonomous driving.

A concrete autonomous enterprise scenario - Order‑to‑Cash as the “autonomous driving” lane : In a first stage, AI improves individual steps: classifying incoming orders, predicting demand, suggesting risk scores, or flagging payment issues. This is similar to Level‑1/2 driver assistance in a car: helpful, but the driver (the user) is still in full control.

With agentic AI on/with SAP BTP and Joule, the pattern shifts: AI agents can monitor the entire O2C flow end‑to‑end, reason over real‑time business data and take actions across systems. For example:

  • An Order Reliability Agent continuously scans open sales orders, checks supply, lead times, logistics constraints and predicts which orders are at risk.

  • If a risk is detected, the agent automatically proposes mitigation options like alternative plants, substitute materials, different carriers or adjusted delivery dates, based on company policies and service levels.

  • A Cash Management or Disputes Agent can, in parallel, reconcile incoming payments, detect anomalies and automatically trigger follow‑ups or dispute workflows, reducing manual reconciliation effort dramatically.news.

From a platform perspective, SAP BTP provides the build, integration, data, workflow and AI services that these agents rely on: APIs to access S/4HANA or CX systems, eventing for business changes, AI engines for prediction and workflow/rules for execution. Joule then acts as the orchestrator and interface: business users can ask natural‑language questions (“Which high‑value orders are at risk this week?”) and approve, override or refine the actions that agents proposes.

This is very close to how autonomous driving stacks are built:

  • Sensors and maps in a car correspond to operational and planning data across ERP, CX and supply chain.

  • The driving policy engine corresponds to business rules, guardrails and objectives (service levels, margin, risk).

  • The autonomous driving controller corresponds to AI agents on SAP BTP and Joule, which continuously sense, decide and act across multiple “lanes” of the enterprise.

The endpoint is not a “hands‑off” company, but an autonomous enterprise where a large share of O2C, source‑to‑pay and plan‑to‑produce runs system‑driven. And people focus on exceptions, design and strategy. Just like a driver in an autonomous vehicle, humans stay in charge of the destination and constraints, while the platform handles the repetitive steering in between.

Legacy Thinking vs. AI- and Autonomy-First Architecture

The key leadership question, in my view, is not: “Where can we quickly add some AI?”

Instead, it is: “What would our product and platform architecture look like if we rebuilt it tomorrow, AI- and autonomy-first, from a clean slate...using the business processes and data we already have?”

A few principles I consider critical, for us and for our customers:

  • Architecture before features: Rethink the platform model first. Data, events, policies and agents...then add capabilities on top.

  • Data and model strategy at the core: Without a clear data foundation, governance and feedback loops, AI and autonomy will stay isolated pilot projects.

  • Platform and ecosystem mindset: Openness, APIs and co-innovation with partners and developers are the lever for scale. No single vendor will “own” all agents or all models.

  • AI embedded, not bolted on: Design AI and agents as a natural layer across processes, data and user journeys, with humans in control and clear guardrails.

  • From workflows to policies and goals: Move from hard-coded workflows towards systems that can optimize against business goals within defined policies. Similar to how autonomous driving optimizes route, speed and safety within traffic rules.

A Question for Platform Leaders on the Road to the Autonomous Enterprise

The automotive industry has learned the hard way what happens when a platform shift is underestimated. In enterprise software, we have the opportunity to avoid repeating that mistake and to use the current AI wave as a stepping stone towards the autonomous enterprise.

If you are responsible for platforms or products...at SAP, on the customer side or in the partner ecosystem, ask yourself two simple questions:

  1. Which parts of your platform would you not build the same way again if you had to start tomorrow, AI-first, from scratch?

  2. What would you need to change so that your systems can not only automate tasks, but autonomously optimize outcomes under clear business guardrails?

That is where your real transformation roadmap begins and the journey we will continue at SAP Sapphire this year, where the next chapter of SAP BTP’s evolution, from intelligent platform to true autonomous enterprise backbone, will come into focus.

Curious to hear:
What would you change in your platform today if your goal was not just automation, but a truly autonomous enterprise tomorrow?

SAP Business Technology Platform, Joule Joule Studio #AutonomousEnterprise SAP Sapphire SAP Business AI

entry.title: SAP Business AI Platform: #1 Autonomous Enterprise - The Vision
entry.content:
"If you understand the vision...it's a strategy.

If you don't understand the vision, it's just a renaming."

At SAP Sapphire 2026 SAP introduced a big term that could redefine how businesses run with AI -  SAP Business AI Platform. 

This is not exactly a rebranding, but SAP Business AI Platform is not entirely a brand new platform either. What SAP is really doing is unifying existing AI capabilities, orchestration layers, agentic workflows, and new experiences under a more connected Business AI and Joule ecosystem.

 

In this blog series, we will explore what SAP Business AI Platform is and why its so important in the world of AI and AI Agents.

This is the first blog, where we will focus on the the big question - Why SAP Business AI Platform is introduced. 
What is SAP's vision of Autonomous Enterprise and how SAP Business AI Platform would support that.

Disclaimer: This blog reflects my personal understanding, interpretation, and point of view. It is not an official publication or statement from SAP.

 

What is the Autonomous Enterprise?

 

Let’s imagine an organization where:

  • AI executes tasks automatically from start to finish.
  • AI detects issues before humans notice them.
  • AI makes decisions within approved boundaries.
  • And still humans stay in control.

That is exactly what we call the Autonomous Enterprise.

In simple words…

An Autonomous Enterprise is a business where:

  • AI assistants and AI agents manage and execute end to end processes,
  • At the same time people stay in control, focusing on judgment, strategy and the value creation that truly matters.

 

A Simple Real-Life Example

 

Humans Are Still in Charge and Important

This does not mean robots are taking over. It means only repetitive operational work is handled by AI.

So that people spend less time navigating systems and coordinating work, and more time focusing on strategic tasks and decisions that truly move the business.

The goal is not replacing people. The goal is amplifying people.

 

Autonomous Enterprise is NOT a New Product

Autonomous Enterprise is NOT a new product or solution.

Autonomous Enterprise is a Vision. It's an end-state for SAP customers to aspire to that only SAP can deliver.

 

5 Key Elements of the Autonomous Enterprise

 

 

1. Joule

Joule as the new engagement layer, bringing together the right data, workflow, and agents across SAP systems and beyond.

Joule will completely reinvent how end-users engage with SAP software and execute via assistants and agents.

 

2. SAP Autonomous Suite

SAP Autonomous Suite is the operational core of the business, organized into Autonomous Domains where people set the direction and assistants and agents execute the work.

The Autonomous Suite is comprised of five autonomous domains:

  1. Autonomous Finance
  2. Autonomous Spend
  3. Autonomous Supply Chain Management (SCM)
  4. Autonomous HCM
  5. Autonomous CX.

Each autonomous domain is comprised of specialized assistants and agents

 

3. Industry AI

Many customers’ most complex and valuable business challenges are highly industry specific. Industry AI extends the autonomous domain model with vertical depth, embedding sector-specific process knowledge, data models, and regulatory logic.  

 

4. SAP Business AI platform

SAP Business AI platform as the new foundation bringing together:

  • SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP)
  • SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC)
  • parts of SAP Business Transformation Management (BTM)
  • and AI Foundation

to deliver business context, unified data, models, and enterprise-grade governance

In the next blog, we will discuss more on what exactly SAP Business AI platform is.

 

5. Accelerate Adoption

For most customers, migration and implementation costs remain the primary barrier to adopting our cloud solutions and moving away from legacy or 3rd party solutions.

To address this, SAP is investing significantly in AI‑assisted business transformation, augmenting the RISE with SAP journey with migration and modernization assistants that fundamentally improve the efficiency of ERP transformation.

Powered by agent-led toolchain and backed by the RISE with SAP Methodology, this approach reduces effort and time, making the path to the cloud more efficient.

For new cloud adopters, SAP GROW pairs pre-configured best practices with AI-assisted implementation for faster time to value.

 

Why Autonomous Enterprise is important?

Most companies are using AI in isolated pockets… a chatbot here, an automation there. But isolated use cases deliver only incremental value.

The real transformation happens when AI is embedded directly into end-to-end business processes, working with trusted business data and enterprise governance.

That is SAP’s vision of the Autonomous Enterprise… where AI does not just answer questions, but actively helps run the business.

Not AI for demos. AI for outcomes. 

Not AI as a feature. AI as the operating model for the future enterprise.


Hope this helped you understand the Autonomous Enterprise. Got any queries, let me know in comment!

Next Blog:

SAP Business AI Platform: #2 What Exactly It is and It's Core Components

entry.title: ABAP AI - Chapter 3: We go agentic!
entry.content:
Summary

  • SAP Joule for developers, ABAP AI capabilities will be supported in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition in all releases as of 2021 with planned start in Q2/2026
  • SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities will be priced on a consumption basis via AI Units
  • ABAP Development Tools for VS Code are planned to be GA in Q2/2026
  • An ABAP MCP Server will be shipped in ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse and ABAP Development Tools for VS Code as of Q2/2026 enabling agentic ABAP development experience
  • An ABAP Custom Code Transformation agent focusing on the migration from SAP ECC towards SAP S/4HANA is planned for Q2/2026

Intro

Since the start of ABAP AI in early 2025 (“Joule speaks ABAP!”), the update in September 2025 (“ABAP AI – Chapter 2”), now it is time to deliver on the promises made at SAP TechEd 2025 (“Introducing the next era of ABAP development”) – time for chapter 3!

We are entering the agentic era of ABAP development: let's get started.

SAP Joule for developers, ABAP AI capabilities in lower releases

As of June 2026, Joule for developers, ABAP AI capabilities are offered through a running on SAP BTP. This hub service combines authentication, license check, access to SAP BTP AI Core and use case specific business logic and can be plugged directly to the developer’s IDE. This way, we can offer ABAP AI capabilities mostly independent of the ABAP backend release. This enables us to serve SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition on releases 2021 and higher with Joule for developers, ABAP AI capabilities.

  • Planned availability in Q2/26
  • Supported releases: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2021 and higher
  • Costs: See below
  • Configuration guide and further information will follow and will be linked here

New commercial approach for ABAP AI

The free promotion for J4D ABAP AI capabilities comes to an end in September 2026. Accordingly, it is now time to launch the commercial model: Based on AI units, ABAP AI capabilities are now priced purely based on consumption. This approach combines transparency regarding the real costs based on the usage and is well-suited for dynamic and individual agentic AI use cases.

  • Planned availability in Q2/26
  • Materials: 8019164 (customers) and 8019670 (partners)
  • Information on how to purchase and activate will follow soon and will be linked here

ABAP Development Tools for VS Code

14 years after the launch of ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse, time has come to support a new IDE. As Q2 2026, ABAP Development Tools will be available for VS Code. Building on the ABAP Language Server (see “Behind the Design…”), coupled with an ABAP MCP Server, this is the enabler for a truly agentic ABAP development experience, boosting development efficiency to the next level. It sets the foundation to leverage modern coding agents like GitHub Copilot directly for ABAP development. While we start with a small set of development objects and IDE tools focusing on SAP Fiori App development based on the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP), we will very soon enhance the scope of object types to serve high priority development scenarios.

The VS Code extension is

  • planned to be available as of Q2/26
  • Supported releases: release 7.31 and higher
  • Works in the Cloud and OnPrem
  • Costs: free of charge
  • Precise info on how to obtain and get started will follow soon and will be linked here

MCP Support for ABAP development

The Language Server provides the required abstraction to gain independence of the IDE sitting on top. A major component of this delivery is the MCP server for ABAP development. It provides powerful tools which help coding agents to perform complex ABAP coding tasks. While the Language Server is currently delivered as part of the ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse and for VS Code, a potential standalone-shipment of the Language and MCP Server is under discussion.

  • Release planned for Q2/26
  • Supported in Eclipse and VS Code
  • Works in the Cloud and OnPrem
  • Supported for release 7.31 and higher
  • Precise info on how to use it in the IDE will follow soon and will be linked here

Agentic custom code migration for the SAP S/4HANA transformation

Bringing all pieces together, we are launching the first ABAP custom code agent assisting in the transformation of custom code from ECC towards SAP S/4HANA. The required capabilities are offered through the ABAP Language and MCP Server explained above and can be used leveraging a coding agent, for example Amazon Q or GitHub Copilot. Currently, Eclipse must be used for this agent, until all required ABAP object types are supported in VS Code (see above). This agent boosts our customers on the RISE journey when migrating their classic landscape to the Cloud ERP.

  • Availability planned for Q2/26.
  • Supported in Eclipse
  • Prerequisites: Joule for developers, ABAP AI capabilities via the new commercial approach with AI units (see above) and coding agent on top (e.g. Amazon Q or Github Copilot)
  • Further information to follow soon and will be linked here

 

Finally - please have a look at our FAQ document for frequent questions and watch out for the GA releases of these deliverables. We will keep you posted.

Get yourself ready to dive into agentic ABAP development! 😎

entry.title: Stop Searching, Start Asking: 3 Ways Joule is Changing the SAP Game!
entry.content:
SAP’s integration of Joule, the generative AI assistant, across Business Transformation Management solutions is quietly reshaping productivity for enterprise teams. Here are 3 actionable insights every SAP user and decision-maker should know:

1️⃣ Be Conversational - Stop hunting for information. Just ask Joule.

Whether you’re working in SAP LeanIX or SAP Signavio, Joule lets you locate process diagrams, fact sheets, reports, and accelerators using plain, everyday language. No complex navigation. No wasted time. Just conversational AI that instantly retrieves what you need.

2️⃣ Be Contextual - Automate routine tasks through chat-based workflows.

From creating and deleting journey models in SAP Signavio to managing to-dos and subscriptions in SAP LeanIX, Joule handles transactional tasks directly through the copilot interface. This isn’t just convenience. It’s a genuine productivity multiplier for your teams.

3️⃣Be Connected - Break down silos between SAP and non-SAP systems.

Powered by WalkMe, the SAP Joule Action Bar proactively engages users based on context, across any application, SAP or non-SAP. This means seamless, always-on AI guidance regardless of where your workflows live.

The future of enterprise productivity can be summed up in 3 Cs:

✔️Conversational — interact naturally, in plain language
✔️Contextual — get guidance tailored to exactly where you are
✔️Connected — work fluidly across every application in your landscape

🤔Which of the 3 Cs do you think would have the biggest immediate impact on your team’s productivity? And why?

entry.title: Top 9 takeaways #SAPSapphire 2026
entry.content:
1. The ERP Brain Has a New Job. It Works for Your Agents Now.
SAP Business AI Platform
 
1. SAP Business AI Platform

 

Almost every company is using AI. Almost none are getting the business value they expected. The Stanford AI survey Christian Klein referenced at the open says what many CIOs already know privately: the problem isn't the model. The problem is everything below the waterline: 7 million data fields, thousands of proprietary processes, authorization rules, governance requirements. None of which any model was trained on, and none of which any model naturally respects.

SAP's answer is the SAP Business AI Platform, launched at Sapphire 2026. Its core is a rich context layer that injects ERP domain knowledge directly into every agent interaction. Knowledge graphs navigate thousands of business processes and select the right data. SAP Domain Models give agents native understanding of business logic. Business Data Cloud provides a single semantic layer across SAP and non-SAP systems. The governance layer checks identity and authorization before any output surfaces.

JPMorgan Chase joined the keynote to make this concrete. The bank processes 12 trillion dollars in payments across 120 currencies every day and is already deploying agentic solutions that catch errors before they post to the ledger. Jeremy Barnum, CFO, JPMorgan Chase:
"AI is only as good as the data and processes underneath it. It can't reach its full potential in a fragmented legacy environment."
The framing is deliberate. SAP is not adding AI to ERP. It is bringing 50 years of ERP domain knowledge into AI. That is a different claim, and a structural one.


2. 224 Agents, 51 Assistants. The Pilot Era is Over.
The Autonomous Suite
 
2. The Autonomous Suite

 

Enterprise AI has lived in pilot purgatory for two years. Impressive demos, limited deployment, unclear ROI. The structural problem is that most agentic platforms are horizontal: they give you a foundation and then require you to build your own business process agents from scratch. SAP is taking the opposite position.

The Autonomous Suite launches with 224 pre-built agents and 51 assistants across five domains: Autonomous Finance, Autonomous Spend, Autonomous Supply Chain, Autonomous HCM, and Autonomous CX. These are outcome-driven, SOX audit-compatible, ISO-certified agents mapped to specific business roles with defined KPIs. Each is extensible: you can add tools, workflow steps, or custom agents for what does not fit out of the box.

Early customers are already in production. Bayer is using the Cash Collections Assistant to accelerate cash flow. Ericsson deployed intelligent role recommendations across 85,000 employees. Petco is using the Merchandising Assistant to surface assortment gaps and optimize product data across all channels. The number of agents and assistants will grow monthly.


3. Company Memory: SAP Decides to Solve the Tribal Knowledge Problem
Company Memory
 
3. Company Memory

 

Every company has two kinds of knowledge. The kind in the system: structured, documented, queryable. And the kind in the heads of the people who have run the process for 15 years and are no longer in the building. The second kind is worth more. It disappears first.

SAP introduced Company Memory as a knowledge management layer for the Autonomous Suite. Built on the Signavio foundation, it ingests process models, policy documents, Teams and Slack channels, and email approval chains. From that unstructured material, it extracts "process atoms": small structured units of business knowledge that tell agents what to look for, what to do, and what not to do. Every override, every pattern, every correction becomes instruction for the next run.

You can directly update any process atom manually, and every agent in your landscape adapts instantly. The system is not learning autonomously without oversight. It is learning with you. The Financial Closing demo illustrated this: 12 legal entities, four ERP systems, six currencies, hundreds of reconciliations. Q2's close was measurably faster than Q1. Each close teaches the next one.


4. Joule Gets a Ground-Up Rebuild. The Internal Name Was V10.
Joule Work
4. Joule Work

 

SAP internally called this the V10 of Joule. The marketing team called it Joule Work instead. The distinction between the current Joule and Joule Work is structural, not incremental.

The current Joule is RAG-based, limited in context and governance. Joule Work introduces a full agentic harness with computer and file access, open standards support including MCP, and a more complete knowledge base. The new Joule Spaces interface creates contextual workspaces that generate dynamically based on the task at hand. SAP's framing is "App-less": an experience layer where users interact with their underlying applications without having to be in them.

The Financial Closing demo gave Joule Spaces its clearest illustration. The Closing Assistant surfaced 18 items requiring human review inside a single workspace: accrual proposals with confidence levels, intercompany variances already resolved, one-click reconciliation across currencies and entities. The human stayed in the loop without ever opening a separate application. That is what "appless" looks like in practice.


5. Build Agents for Outcomes, Not Architecture: Joule Studio 2.0
Joule Studio 2.0
 
5. Joule Studio 2.0

 

The standard criticism of agentic AI tools is that they ask the builder to think like an engineer when the business needs them to think like a process owner. Joule Studio 2.0 is designed to invert that sequence.

The new Studio is intent-based: you describe the business challenge, not the technical specification. Philip Herzig demonstrated this with a pharma distribution scenario: margin leakage of $24 million identified via SAP Signavio. Joule Studio took that diagnosis, generated a full product requirements document, identified two existing agents already covering part of the workflow, and built the full orchestration without writing a single line of code. KPMG is targeting $120 million in reduced contract leakage using this stack.

Joule Studio 2.0 is model-agnostic: bring any third-party LLM or SAP's own. Development is low-code, pro-code, or anything in between. The evaluation framework is data-driven, so you validate agent behavior against actual business outcomes. It is available free through end of year.


6. Industry AI: The Superpower SAP Has Been Sitting On
Industry AI
 
6. Industry AI

 

Sebastian Steinhauser opened with a raised hand. Who is using SAP 100% in standard? Almost none. Who is using SAP with industry-specific solutions? Most of the room. That gap has historically been filled by customizations and the expertise of people who have been in the industry for 20 years.

Industry AI is SAP's move to build that depth in. Seven autonomous industry solutions are launching now, spanning adaptive production, project delivery, regulated manufacturing, revenue growth management, and unified commerce. Take regulated manufacturing: in pharma, every batch must be traced and audit-ready. The Industry AI solution delivers multi-agent processes that detect disruptions early and simulate compliant recovery options in real time. Takeda reports up to 25% reduction in revenue loss from stockouts.

H&M offered the fullest picture. 4,100 stores across 81 markets, with a Store Intelligence Agent coordinating end-to-end across the value chain in real time. Ellen Swanstrom, Chief Digital and Information Officer, H&M:
"To unlock the full potential of new technologies like AI, the right digital foundation is needed to ensure that we can standardize and harmonize key business processes. And SAP sits at the core of our processes."


7. Below the Waterline: Dremio, Prior Labs, and SAP-RPT-1.5
Business Data Cloud
 
7. Business Data Cloud

 

Three data announcements in fifteen minutes. Any one of them would have been the headline at a smaller event.

SAP intends to acquire Dremio, a high-performance data lakehouse with federation across any cloud and legacy database. Combined with Business Data Cloud, this enables agent reasoning across any cloud environment without data movement. SAP also intends to acquire Prior Labs, whose TabPFN model family handles non-SAP structured data for prediction use cases including financial trading. And SAP is launching SAP-RPT-1.5, the next generation of its own tabular foundation model, with a chat API for what-if analysis, predictive simulation, and built-in explainability, without any model training required.

Together, SAP-RPT-1.5 and TabPFN address the part of enterprise AI that large language models were never designed for: accurate, explainable, prediction-grade outcomes on tabular business data. The LLM is the tip of the iceberg. The data layer being assembled here is everything underneath it.


8. One Command Center for Every Agent You Didn't Build: AI Agent Hub
AI Agent Hub
 
8. AI Agent Hub

 

By now, most large enterprises have more AI agents running than anyone has officially counted. Some are great. Some are not. Almost no one has a consistent picture of which agents are operating, what they are allowed to do, and whether they comply with governance requirements.

SAP AI Agent Hub, built on LeanIX (already used by more than 25% of the Fortune 500), is the central command center for all of it. Teams can discover, manage, and govern SAP agents, non-SAP agents, and MCP servers across the landscape. Telemetry routes to Cloud ALM for health monitoring. Signavio's agent mining surfaces optimization opportunities against KPIs. All agent activity maps to SuccessFactors so the workforce impact is visible end-to-end.

Three things worth noting: governing non-SAP agents is free. A2A interoperability with third-party agentic platforms is also free. And the full governance lifecycle is included in SAP Business AI Platform at no extra charge. The governance infrastructure is being given away. The value proposition is the agents running on top of it.


9. Rise and Grow with SAP: Change Management Written Into the Deal
Rise and Grow with SAP
 
9. Rise and Grow with SAP

 


The keynote offered a clear framing: "Technology alone and plugging AI agents into your existing system landscape will drive zero value." That framing is deliberate. Rise and Grow with SAP is built to answer it directly.

The new Rise and Grow with SAP offering responds directly. SAP commits to sending architects, AI developers, and industry consultants on-site to drive change management and AI adoption, regardless of whether the customer starts on S/4HANA or ECC. The contractual commitment: activate three dual assistants of your choice within year one. For migration, Joule for developers and Joule for consultants are being extended with AI assistants targeting a 50% reduction in total migration effort.

The tooling behind that commitment is now visible. Seven specialized migration assistants, covering system analysis, data management, custom code, configuration, test management, rollout, and project management, are orchestrated inside Cloud ALM. Joule for Consultants is the single entry point across all of them. System analysis that previously required weeks of preparation can now run over a weekend.

Technology. Change management. Migration. These are the three failure modes of large-scale ERP transformation, and SAP is now naming all three in the same commercial offer. The argument is not that SAP's AI is the best AI. It is that no matter where you start, SAP will make you ready to use AI tomorrow.

entry.title: Sapphire 2026. The future of UX is here. Are you ready for an AI -powered world?
entry.content:
Sapphire 2026 accelerated SAP’s pivot to AI. Most of the keynote demos were heavy on AI, including introducing new engagement layer experiences to complement the existing application experiences. The magic of the engagement layer is that it allows you to interact with your ERP system in a conversational way, starting with your intent and the system will generate an interface based on the context of your request. While this might represent a more natural way to work, it also represents a major change to how people have been working with SAP applications for the past few decades.

The new engagement layer lets you interact with SAP in a conversational way.

I had the opportunity to see many deep-dive sessions that explained the new UX and how we expect people to work with it. More importantly, I spoke with many customers and partners about their reaction to the way we are moving. Generally, people are excited about the direction we are going and having more automation to free up time for deeper thought and analysis, but they are concerned about trusting AI to do things that might have significant business impact. While Joule Work will certainly be a game changer for many, the big open question is how to get there. As with all IT transformations, it will involve a combination of changes across people, processes, and technology.

Embedded AI meets customers where they are

The first session that I presented was on “Creating better user experiences with AI-powered SAP Fiori apps ASUG.” I started out by talking about overcoming usability challenges with AI and then did a live demo of around 10 cool productivity features. What’s unique about the embedded AI capabilities in SAP Cloud ERP is that it is built into the existing interfaces that people know, so it allows them to go through their work more quickly by using AI to search, summarize, and enter data.

There are many AI-powered features that make people more productive when using SAP Cloud ERP.

The slides from the session (PUX1228) include links where you can see short recorded demos of these features. These capabilities are available for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. Some of them are coming to private cloud in upcoming releases.

Some questions that customers asked that might be of general interest:

  • Does the engagement layer replace SAP Fiori? No – the application experience and engagement layer experience are complementary. They will coexist for the foreseeable future. (Thanks for the question, Tony)
  • Does the engagement layer use SAP Fiori elements? The answer here is more nuanced. We use the OData metadata that is used by Fiori elements to get the SAP S/4HANA data to render high quality cards in the engagement layer.
    So, the engagement layer is independent of the Fiori apps themselves. The engagement layer does not call the Fiori apps for the data, but it uses the same underlying data source, which are the OData services and annotations. (Great question, Kelly)

Lots of people were interested in having a better user experience with AI assistance.

Fear, optimism, and the AI Skills gap

My other presentation was a “community conversation” in the ASUG Hub. My colleague, Nicole Smythe and I facilitated the session along with Liz Tuckwell and Nadalee Williams from ASUG. The goal was to collect ideas on AI adoption from the attendees and equip them with information so they could create a 90-day plan to turn their thoughts into action and move forward with AI in your organization. After providing an overview of Joule Work, we broke into teams and people discussed barriers to implementing AI in their organizations and how they overcame them.

Top problems that the audience identified included:

  • Data. How to get the data clean so any AI agents can work properly and consistently. Most people know they need to fix their business processes and clean their data before they can benefit from AI.
  • Governance. People see the need for IT to manage this centrally. Currently, many innovative teams want to get started with AI and are doing things on their own without corporate oversight. We saw this situation a decade ago when some business managers purchased cloud services for sales or HR or other domains if they did not receive sufficient support from their IT teams.
  • Cost. How to meter AI consumption and allocate charges to different users.
  • AI Expertise. Upskilling teams on AI and its capabilities. Currently, this is a side job for most people, so they are not putting enough focus on this. Once company had a rotation program where top developers could allocate 50% of their time to learning AI topics and then bring those skills back to their teams.
  • Talent management. With fewer entry-level workers or people doing repetitive jobs, it is harder to identify the best employees, so you can focus on retention and building a leadership pipeline.

Participants discussed how to overcome barriers to AI adoption.

Other interesting sessions on AI, UX, and AI+UX

Most of the other sessions I attended were on AI, UX, or their intersection. Hearing the questions people asked and what they talked about afterwards will be helpful in refining our agenda for our next SAP Connect Day for UX, which is in Palo Alto on June 11. We’ll go deep on our UX strategy and share some new functionality, both released and unreleased. There will be two hands-on exercises, one on building applications and the other building Smart Helpers (currently in beta).

  • Katja Zoch and Sebastian Steinhauer had a session on how to extend SAP S/4HANA Cloud while keeping a clean core. They focused on what is clean core, why its important, and how to extend your core ERP system using clean core principles.  
  • Chris Hoffmann had several sessions on the show floor. They all focused on different dimensions of the AI-powered user experience in the SAP Business Suite. One was a broad survey of functionality, another focused on AI-assisted task automation, and the other talked about roadmap items. All showed how AI adoption is on the path towards human‑supervised autonomous ERP.
  • Tom Reiss presented on SAP Fiori in action, highlighting how modern UX helps users get their work done more easily and efficiently. He also had a roadmap session with Christina Salwizek featuring web and mobile innovations that are on the horizon.
  • Sophia Levens had some great sessions on the new Joule user experience, focusing on both how it works and the implications for users. She also had a strategy-focused talk with SAP’s chief designer officer, Arin Bhowmick, where they explored where user engagement and enterprise UX are headed.
  • Bert Schultz and Esther Vrieze spoke about different ways to migrate to SAP S/4HANA and the relative benefits among the different approaches.
  • Maura Hameroff and Rudi Hois talked about SAP Cloud ERP Private and the many innovations that customers can expect to see in upcoming releases.

We held our SAP Fiori Development Roundtable live from the show

To add a little extra energy to our already lively discussions, I joined my co-host Sebastian Werner live from the show floor. We spent lots of time talking about Joule Studio and how it fits with our existing development tools. If you want to join the next monthly call, please register.

AI vibe from across the conference

While the sessions are a great way to learn about hot topics, the best way to hear about how customers are using our solutions and what they are planning is in the hallways, food areas, UXpresso Café (successor to the Fiori Café), happy hours, and demo pods. The questions after sessions are also revealing. This year, the customers I spoke to were concerned about:

  • How the new engagement layer (Joule Work) fits with SAP Fiori
  • How to get started with AI, especially in private cloud and on-prem environments
  • If their existing Fiori apps will continue to work (yes!) and if they should continue to build new Fiori apps (yes!)
  • All the topics listed above from the ASUG community conversation (data, governance, cost, AI skills, and talent pipeline)

Next steps

Perhaps you are still digesting all the announcements from Sapphire and figuring out their impact on your business. To help you prepare for the future, consider discussing the following questions with your team.

  1. What business problems are most pressing and can benefit from automation? How will we meter the costs, set appropriate limits, and measure the return on investment?
  2. Is our data clean, consistent across different parts of the business, ready to train the AI system, and be part of automated workflows? How much autonomy are we willing to give agents and what is the best way to supervise / manage / audit their activities?
  3. How will we integrate AI into our SAP landscape? Are we using SAP’s embedded capabilities, Joule, the new engagement layer, or external AI-powered apps running on SAP data (subject to the API policy)?
  4. How will we address change management with our business users?
  5. How will we manage AI governance, risk, and security? Using SAP’s AI simplifies the answer to this question as we cover much of this, but you still need robust AI policies in your organization

If we did not meet at Sapphire and you are interested in joining the conversation about SAP Fiori application development and how AI makes it better, faster, and cheaper, please join our monthly SAP Fiori development roundtable group or sign up for our bi-monthly newsletter.

If you are in the Silicon Valley area or can fly there on June 11, please join us for SAP Connect Day for UX. We'll go deeper into all the UX and related AI topics listed in this post.
https://events.sap.com/us-ux-innovation-day-siliconvalley-2026/en_us/home.html 

For the UX Foundation team, Peter Spielvogel.

entry.title: SAP UX Update: The New Joule Work Engagement Layer Experiences
entry.content:
What is Joule Work, and what does it mean for you? What do the new Joule Work engagement layer experiences mean for existing application experiences? How can you try it out? Read on to get an overview of Joule Work and answers to these questions.

What is Joule Work?

Joule Work provides an AI-based user experience that transforms how work happens. With Joule work, people express what they want to accomplish, and Joule surfaces the right insights, automates routine work, and coordinates AI agents across systems to achieve it – all in one dynamic workspace. It acts as an engagement layer that spans across SAP products and beyond, and coexists with current experiences provided by pre-defined application UIs.

It represents a new way of interacting with business systems, since it provides:

  • One single place where a user can get tasks done, rather than the traditional app-centric interaction model, where users need to navigate to different applications or even to different products.
  • An intent-driven, situational experience, where users state their intents in natural language and the system builds on the fly accordingly, taking the current business and user context (the current situation) into account: fetching data, triggering AI agents, and visualizing the outcome appropriately.

Figure 1: Joule Work experiences: Conversations, Spaces, and Develop. Alt Text: The image shows 3 screenshots, one each for the three experiences. The top of the image has a heading  “Joule Work”, above a row with five attributes: Dynamic, Proactive, Agentic, Intent-based, Omnipresent.

Joule Work provides these experiences:

  • Conversations: this experience allows people to express intent and explore what they want to accomplish through dialogue with Joule. It is an AI-powered chat supporting role-based intents, multiple conversation threads, with type-ahead, auto-suggestions and multi-facetted types of responses (text, tables, charts etc.). Joule brings together guidance and outcomes from across SAP and third-party systems. Conversations can also act as an entry point to Spaces for more complex workflows.
  • Spaces: provides flexible AI-generated spaces that dynamically adapt content based on user intent and context, for dealing with simple as well as complex tasks and issues. Each space is composed in real time to bring the right data, tools, and context together for a specific goal, allowing people to work in an environment that adapts to their present goal, instead of navigating applications and their pre-defined UIs.​
  • Develop: this experience is provided by Joule Studio, enabling developers to vibe-code, test and deploy AI agents, applications, and automations across SAP and third-party systems. Joule Work provides a convenient entry point to Joule Studio for developers and business users alike, giving them an AI-powered user experience for pro-code and low-code tools.

To get a better idea of these experiences, have a look at Joule Work in action:

Click on the image to start the video.

Joule Work mobile app

The Joule Work mobile app provides Joule Work Conversations and Spaces for mobile phones and tablets. Figure 2 shows some examples.

Figure 2: Joule Work mobile app, examples of Conversations and Spaces. Alt Text: two pairs of iPhone images. On the left Joule Work Conversations, showing a start and a screen listing some return orders and a user prompt. On the right a screen with text “Create a new space” and a user input “Create a space to sow my open customer returns for BikePro”, and a screen showing the resulting space with a chart, some cards with recommended tasks, and a return order analysis.

Find out more here. 

  • Joule Work mobile app is here!

What are the benefits for businesses and their users?

Businesses expect that the new AI/agentic, intent-driven way of working that is taking hold across the industry is ushering in a new era of user interaction and user experience, that will bring them gains in productivity and data quality, along with better business insights and outcomes, while saving training costs and reducing user-support costs.

Joule Work from SAP has an ingrained understanding of the customer’s business processes: hence it is best placed to deliver on the above promise. It can provide experiences that bring together the right data and services from across our applications to guide users from initial intent to a successful outcome.

It helps users get their work done faster, more easily, and with better outcomes, by:

  • Providing one place to get their work done, so they don’t have to navigate to many different applications and products, and don’t have to navigate through layers of application UIs, thus reducing the number of clicks and interactions significantly.
  • Allowing users to state their intent in natural language with a conversational interface, then triggering the relevant actions to achieve their intent, and showing the outcome(s) in a personalized way, tailored to the user’s needs.

Which target groups of users and use cases is Joule Work meant for?

Business and technical users. The goal is to help all our customer’s SAP users get their work done more easily, focusing on outcomes, so it is not limited to specific target groups.

The Joule Work Develop experience is provided by Joule Studio. Find out more about the new version of Joule Studio in this news article:

  • Announcing New Joule Studio for Enterprise Scale Agentic Development.

What does this mean for existing UIs and SAP Fiori apps?

Existing SAP products and applications built with SAP’s UI technology stack, as well as other apps in our portfolio, will continue to progress with their roadmap and planned product strategy. Joule Work has no impact on existing UIs, instead it complements them. Customers will continue to get the benefits from existing applications and their investment is protected.

Joule Work provides an additional option for users to interact with the system. Wherever they find that it helps them get tasks done more easily and/or with better outcomes, they are likely to do those tasks with Joule Work. Wherever they feel that their familiar, pre-defined applications are better suited to a task, they will continue using them.

This means that SAP delivers two kinds of experiences:

  • Application experiences (from SAP Cloud ERP, SAP SuccessFactors, SAP Analytics Cloud, etc.): SAP and customer-built transactional and analytical application experiences.
  • Engagement layer experiences (Joule Work experiences): AI-native, agentic, and generative experiences that help users interact with and orchestrate work across those applications.

Our users are used to application UIs and pre-defined navigation paths, how to manage the transition?

The existing product home pages and cross-product entry points such as SAP Build Work Zone and Joule Work mobile app (formerly called SAP Mobile Start) continue to be available for providing pre-defined as well as customer-defined pages and navigation menus. Similarly, the existing applications continue to be available, with their application experiences.

Users working in the web browser can open Joule Work from Joule and hence start getting familiar with the new way of working. Since the new experience co-exists with the current experience it will be easy for users to adapt and adopt. We expect the transition to be user-led, in that they will choose whichever experience helps them get specific tasks done more easily and/or with better outcomes.

If users are already familiar with popular consumer applications such as ChatGPT, Google Gemini or Claude Sonnet, then they will be able to build on this to leverage the next generation user experience provided by Joule Work, with the new ways of interacting.

Does it complement or does it replace existing solutions such as SAP Start, SAP Build Work Zone, and SAP Mobile Start?

It complements them. The existing solutions allow customer administrators to pre-define layouts, content and navigation options, which helps users access applications and information using familiar pages, menus and links. It makes it easy for them to understand the site and feel comfortable using it in their daily work. This is something that benefits all users but is especially helpful for users new to the system to get started quickly and get familiar with their work environment.

SAP Mobile Start has been rebranded to Joule Work mobile app, accompanied by a new app icon and an updated user experience. For customers previously accessing Joule within SAP Mobile Start, the Joule entry point has transitioned from the diamond in the header bar to a full-screen interface to be accessed via the tab bar. All core functionality and features remain unchanged, ensuring continuity for both customers and end users with no disruption to existing workflows. In addition to the core functionality, it provides the Joule Work engagement layer experience for mobile devices. Find out more here:

  • SAP Mobile Start - Rebranding of the app, enhanced Joule and improved single sign-on functionality.

For SAP Cloud ERP Private: would we need to go to SAP Fiori (implement SAP Fiori launchpad), or can we skip it and go to Joule Work directly?

You would need to implement the SAP Fiori launchpad for Joule Work to work with SAP Cloud ERP Private: they use the navigation and authorization information defined by customers in the SAP Fiori launchpad to access the system. Also, Joule only runs in the browser on desktops and is launched from the launchpad. Similarly, if users navigate from Joule Work to applications, this navigation will be to browser-based applications running in the launchpad.

How can partners and customers extend standard SAP functionality for Joule Work – as a key user or as a developer?

The established programming model for creating extensions continues to be valid. Joule Work accesses OData services and their meta-data (amongst others) and hence will also access extensions or custom OData services that have been developed according to the SAP BTP Developer’s Guide.

Using Joule Studio or SAP Build is the way to build extensions and agents, for Joule Work as well as for pre-defined applications.

If you are building new applications, follow the SAP BTP Developer’s Guide, and use Joule Studio or SAP Build. You can build agents on top of the APIs you create, and Joule Work will access the OData services and meta-data of your new applications. The Fiori apps you create using SAPUI5 / SAP Fiori elements will allow users to use your new functionality using apps with pre-defined UIs, an experience that they are familiar with. Also, users can use these UIs to confirm that what was done by Joule Work is indeed correct and hence build trust in it.

What is available today (Sapphire 2026)

As described above, this first version of Joule Work provides Conversations, Spaces, and Develop, with the Joule Work Develop experience being provided by Joule Studio. The Sapphire scope:

  • Supports integration to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and SAP SuccessFactors.
