  • Supports exactly one SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, you cannot connect to more than one.
  • Does not support document grounding in Joule.

How can customers get Joule Work, and what are the pre-requisites?

If you are interested, you can sign up to the limited Early Adopter Care (EAC) program. The pre-requisites for this at the time of writing are as follows:

  • Joule needs to be SAP-managed.
    • New SAP-managed Joule instances are supported
    • Existing customers with customer-managed Joule would need to transition to SAP-managed Joule.
  • Available only in specific regions: USA and EU.

Final words

I hope you are as excited as I am about the huge potential these Joule Work experiences have for helping our users get their work done faster and more easily, with better outcomes. It will be interesting to observe how people in various roles adopt this new way of working, and how fast they adopt: for which tasks do users prefer to use Joule Work, for which ones do they continue to prefer application experiences – and why?

If you want to dig a bit deeper, have a look at how we are designing these new experiences, and see how we are evolving the SAP Design System for AI-driven UX:

  • Designing the Autonomous Enterprise.
  • The Compositional Design System.
  • AI and Design.

If you want an overview of interesting UX shown at Sapphire in Orlando this week, have a look at  Peter Spielvogel's nice summary:

  • Sapphire 2026. The future of UX is here. Are you ready for an AI -powered world?

entry.title: Manufacturing Update in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602.3 (HFC10)
entry.content:
Production planners and supervisors can now leverage the power of agentic AI to release production orders faster and with greater confidence — automating prerequisite checks, identifying bottlenecks, and proposing rescheduling options, all within a single guided workflow.

AI-Assisted Production Order Release with the Production Planning and Operations Agent

 

Why This Matters for Production Planning

Releasing production orders is a critical and often time-intensive step in the manufacturing execution process. Planners must validate multiple conditions — material availability, capacity, scheduling feasibility — before a release can safely proceed. Errors or oversights at this stage can result in production delays, resource conflicts, and downstream disruptions.

By embedding AI directly into this workflow, SAP enables a new level of decision support. The Production Planning and Operations Agent does not replace the planner — it handles the verification groundwork so the planner can focus on judgment calls. The result is faster release cycles, fewer manual errors, and more confident decision-making.

This capability is a tangible step toward the intelligent, autonomous supply chain that SAP envisions through its suite of AI and Joule-powered experiences in SAP S/4HANA Cloud.

What Is the Production Planning and Operations Agent?

The Production Planning and Operations Agent is a Joule-powered AI capability embedded directly into the Manage Production Orders app in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. It introduces a new Release button in the app's worklist, enabling planners to initiate an AI-guided production order release process — handling up to 20 orders at a time.

Joule Release Button

Rather than manually verifying each order's readiness, the agent takes over the time-consuming prerequisite checks and surfaces actionable insights so that planners can focus on decisions, not data gathering.

Key Capabilities

Component Availability Checks 

Automatically verifies that all required components are available or identifies suitable alternatives before release.

 Work Center Capacity Checks

Evaluates work center load and capacity constraints to ensure orders can be executed without creating bottlenecks.

Rescheduling

When prerequisites are not met, the agent proposes concrete rescheduling options so production can proceed on a revised timeline.

Bulk Release — Up to 20 Orders

Process multiple production orders in a single agent-guided session, dramatically reducing manual effort for planners. 

How It Works: Step by Step

1) Open the Manage Production Orders App

Navigate to the Manage Production Orders app and select the orders you want to release from the worklist.

2) Initiate the Agent via the Release Button

Click the new Release button to start the Production Planning and Operations Agent. The agent immediately begins analyzing the selected orders.

3) Automated Prerequisite Checks

The agent runs component availability checks (including alternatives), validates work center capacity, and identifies any orders that are not yet ready for release.

 4) Review Proposals and Rescheduling Suggestions

For orders with unmet prerequisites, the agent presents rescheduling proposals. The planner reviews and decides which changes to accept.

5) Confirm and Release

Once all criteria are met and the planner confirms the proposed changes, the agent completes the production order release — updating the system accordingly.

 Activation and Prerequisites

This feature is not enabled by default. It must be activated through the Activate Features configuration activity using the following feature flag: PP_FIO_SFC_AI297_RELEASEAGENT

For detailed activation steps, refer to the Activate Features documentation in your SAP S/4HANA Cloud system. Note: The Release button is only available when SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition is integrated with Joule. Ensure your Joule integration is configured before activating this feature.

Entitlement Notice:

To access this generative AI feature within SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, an additional entitlement and authorization may be required. Please consult your SAP account executive for more information.

Required Authorizations

To access the Manage Production Orders app and use the AI-assisted release capability, business users must have the following business catalogue SAP_SCM_BC_PRODN_ORD_CTRL_MC assigned. 

Enjoy exploring this new AI feature in the world of manufacturing. 

Let me know your feedback below. 

Br, Markus

entry.title: SAP JOULE Agent & ME : My First JOULE Agent in SAP EHSM-Incident Management Without Coding
entry.content:
This blog outlines the basic features of SAP JOULE Agent setup for validating EHS Incident location, and creating EHS Incident, and retrieving EHS Incidents summary. I have leveraged SAP JOULE`s low-code  and no-code capabilities to build an impactful solution.

"A near miss reported today is an accident prevented tomorrow"...

If you want to gain a nuanced understanding of the Joule Skill and Process Automation setup, use the link below:

  • SAP JOULE & ME : My First JOULE Skill in SAP EHSM-Incident Management Without Coding 
  • SAP Joule Skill for Technical Completion of Maintenance Orders in S/4HANA Asset Management 
  • SAP Build Process Automation(SBPA):S4HANA Asset Management-Automating Major Maintenance Work Request 

What`s the difference between a Joule skill and Joule Agent?:

  • Joule Skill: It executes predefined and repetitive single operations with known inputs and outputs.
  • Joule Agent: It focuses on problem solving with multi-step complex strategy.

(In my view, JOULE Skill is like a hand that executes what it is told, and JOULE Agent is like a brain that decides which skill to be used, in what order, and under what conditions.)

Pre-requisites for a Joule Agent:

  • Joule Skills that will be used in the Agent.

Joule Agent Blueprint:

Joule agent`s behavior is driven by a prompt. Post receiving  the prompt, the agent goes through the set of instructions, context, and tool definitions that joule studio has assembled , and empowers the LLMs(Large Language Models) to automate the tasks and drives business outcomes. The clarity of the prompt is the most critical factor in determining the Joule Agent`s reliability and performance. The key components of Joule Agent are:

  • Expertise: It defines agent`s role.
  • Instructions: It specifies the agent`s primary and non-negotiable task.
  • Additional Context: It specifies rules and conditional logic.
  • Tool Descriptions: Defines the available tools and acts as the agent`s manual for its capabilities.

Key Principles for the Agent setup:

  • Define a clear goal and the required steps to reach it.
  • We must break the complex rules into simple, ordered lists.
  • We must ensure that every step leads to a clear action.
  • Avoid ambiguity.
  • Grant access to the required tools for the agent`s job.
  • We must maintain the consistency in the instructions.

Exception Management: Joule Agents are designed for complex tasks, which means they need to be tested and validated carefully. Results are not always the same for the same input and behavior can vary depending on the model and configuration. Building a reliable agent usually take a few iterations, with clear instructions, well-scoped inputs, and the right tools. Agent`s don`t think like humans, and in some cases a joule skill  may be a better fit. So it`s important to understand when to use each.

My Business Use Case:

Our organization`s policy is that every employee can report an EHS Incident (Incident, Near Miss, Safety Observation). To support this, we have setup seamless integration between SAP Asset Management and SAP EHSM modules.

Each equipment is treated as an EHS Location and is assigned with a unique "Technical Identification Number". Our employees typically  recognize the equipment by this technical ID, rather than the SAP Equipment Code (Internal Number), which is harder to remember. So, our EHS Incident Agent helps employees in creating an EHS Incident by retrieving and validating the EHS Location ID linked to the Equipment by using the Equipment Technical Identification Number.

Come on in...let us experience the SAP Joule Agent`s capabilities, It really is the bee`s knees...

Welcome to the SAP Joule Agent`s show...

 

To achieve my business use case, I have created two Joule Agents, one agent is responsible for retrieving the EHS location that is integrated with an Equipment, and the second agent is responsible for creating an EHS Incident.

Joule Skills mentioned below are wrapped in for an EHS Incident Agent to retrieve EHS Location integrated with an Equipment.

Joule Skills mentioned below are wrapped in for an EHS Incident Agent for creating an EHS Incident using user`s natural language prompt.

Let us setup Joule Agents : EHS Incident Location Identifier Agent and EHS Incident Creation Agent.

For Instance, I have chosen LLM Provider as "Anthropic" and Planning Model  & Response Model as "Claude Sonnet 4".

Assign the required Joule Skills under "Tools" tab:

Mini Note: While assigning the Joule Skills, we must consider that, the more tools an agent has, the more complex its decision-making becomes. This can lead to wrong tool choices, slower performance. The bad practice is granting an agent access to all the available tools. A good practice is granting agent access only to the specific tools it needs to perform its job.

EHS Incident Creation Joule Agent:

Once the Joule Agent setup is done, we can test the agent capability using  the option "Test" and release and deploy in the respective environment. For Instance, I have created a private environment for testing my Joule Agent. Let us test the Joule agent capabilities.

Let us first retrieve the EHS Incident details:

Let us create an EHS Incident.

Mini Note: Since the OData demands the date in the UTC format, I have Instructed the agent to consider the current date and time in UTC format if user does not input the date.

Once we are satisfied with the Joule Agent behaviour, we can deploy the Joule Agent. For Instance, I have deployed my Joule Agents in the "Asset Management Environment".

Let us launch the "Joule".

 

 

 

 

Let us check the Incident in the EHSM-Incident Management using Manage Incident Application.

Now let us retrieve the EHS Incident details.

As part of my instructions, my Joule Agent will prompt user to provide the date range for the retrieval process.

Once user provides the date range, Joule Agent fetches the details and displays the summary by Incident category.

Key Takeaways:

  • Separate Joule Agents by clear responsibilities. For Example: Retrieving EHS Incident Location ID details and Creating an EHS Incident.
  • Keep Joule Agents lean and specialized for better performance.
  • Overloading Joule Agent with more Joule Skills can increase complexity , bring decision making capability down, reduces reliability.

Mini Note:

If you want to launch these two agents quickly or want to try them in your own landscape, they are officially available on SAP Business Accelerator Hub. You can access these pre-built templates to fast-track your setup.

 

SAP Sapphire 2026 Innovation Guide:

The biggest takeaway from SAP Sapphire 2026 is , Joule has officially evolved from a simple text chatbot into a comprehensive orchestration layer for complex business processes. SAP is introducing  a unified, intent-driven ecosystem with JOULE WORK.

Please check the link below for a deep dive into the Joule Work at SAP Sapphire 2026...

Meet Joule: The future of work 

&

If you want to gain knowledge on the seamless integration of EHSM Locations and Asset Management, checkout my blog post link below.

SAP S/4HANA EHS : Environment Management (Emissions Management) – Part:1 (Master Data Entities) 

 

😊This brings me to the end of the SAP Joule Agent setup process walkthrough. I hope my blog post proves helpful for the SAP functional consultants begin their journey with the SAP Joule Agents using Low Code -  No Code strategy.

Stay tuned for more updates on SAP Joule Agents in Asset Management and EHSM space...

--

Yours Sincerely,

Pardhasaradhi Reddy. Chelikam

entry.title: SAP Joule Document Grounding with Google Drive - Connecting AI to Enterprise Knowledge (Video Demo)
entry.content:
Enterprise AI becomes significantly more powerful when it can access organization-specific knowledge instead of relying only on generic model responses.

With document grounding in SAP Joule, Joule can retrieve information from enterprise repositories such as Google Drive or SharePoint and generate contextual, reliable responses using Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG).

In this blog, I’ll demonstrate how to connect SAP Joule with Google Drive and enable document grounding using SAP BTP.


What is Document Grounding?

Document grounding allows Joule to retrieve enterprise documents before generating responses.

This helps:

  • Improve response accuracy
  • Reduce hallucinations
  • Provide organization-specific answers
  • Show source references for transparency

Typical grounded content includes:

  • SOPs
  • Functional specifications
  • HR policies
  • IT procedures
  • Internal process documentation

Demo Scenario

For this demo:

  • Google Drive is used as the document repository
  • SAP Joule is connected through the Document Grounding service
  • A RAG pipeline indexes enterprise documents
  • Joule retrieves information directly from grounded documents

I tested two Joule instances:

  • One without grounding
  • One with document grounding enabled

Steps to Enable Document Grounding in SAP Joule

Prerequisites

Ensure you have:

  • Access to SAP Joule
  • Document Grounding service entitlement
  • Google Cloud access
  • Google Drive repository containing documents

Step 1 — Subscribe to SAP Joule and Grounding Service

In your SAP BTP subaccount:

  1. Subscribe to:
    • SAP Joule
    • Document Grounding service

  2. Once the grounding service is available:
    • Create a service instance
    • Generate a service key

The service key will be used for authentication while creating and managing the RAG pipeline.

Step 2 — Create a Google Cloud Service User

In Google Cloud Platform:

  1. Create a Service Account
  2. Generate credentials for the service account
  3. Download the JSON credentials file

This service user will allow the grounding service to access Google Drive content securely.

Step 3 — Create a Google Drive Folder and Grant Access

  1. Create a dedicated folder in Google Drive

Example:
SAPJouleDataGrounding

  1. Upload the documents you want Joule to access:
    • PDFs
    • PPTs
    • Text files
    • Images

  2. Grant View access on the folder to the Google Cloud service user created earlier.

This allows the grounding pipeline to index and retrieve content from the repository.

Step 4 — Create and Verify the Grounding Pipeline

Using Postman or any API testing tool:

  1. Authenticate using the grounding service key
  2. Create a new grounding/RAG pipeline
  3. Configure:
    • Google Drive as the repository
    • Folder details
    • Service account credentials
  4. Activate the pipeline

Once created, verify the pipeline status.

In my demo, the pipeline successfully served content from the Google Drive folder, confirming:

  • Repository connectivity
  • Successful indexing

Step 5 — Test in SAP Joule

Now launch SAP Joule and ask organization-specific questions.

Example:

“What’s the process to raise SE38 FFID?”

or

“Tell me about Auralis custom process for vendor onboarding.”

With document grounding enabled, Joule retrieves the relevant enterprise documents and generates contextual responses along with source references

Demo Video

I have also included a dedicated demo video showcasing:

  • End-to-end setup of document grounding
  • Google Drive integration
  • RAG pipeline creation and verification
  • Grounded vs ungrounded Joule responses
  • Enterprise knowledge retrieval in action using SAP Joule

The video provides a complete walkthrough of the configuration process along with a live demonstration of grounded AI responses.

Thank you for reading this blog.

I will also be creating more content around custom SAP Joule Agents using Joule Studio and sharing additional demos, integrations, and enterprise AI use cases here.

Stay tuned for more SAP Joule and AI-related content.

entry.title: Building an Autonomous SAP Joule Agent for ServiceNow ITSM on SAP BTP
entry.content:
Moving on from the two very basic custom Joule Skills created in the previous post — which could only create and update ServiceNow incidents with a very limited and hardcoded set of input values — this blog takes a much bigger leap toward a fully autonomous custom SAP Joule Agent.

This custom AI-powered Joule Agent, which I call ServiceNow ITSM Expert, leverages the power of an MCP server built for ServiceNow using OpenAPI specifications for ServiceNow APIs and deployed on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP).

The agent provides all the capabilities required to interact intelligently with ServiceNow incidents, including:

Creating incidents
Updating incidents
Searching incidents
Listing incidents
Copying incidents
Maintaining incident quality and categorization automatically

The simplified architecture looks like this:

 

While the technical integration itself is powerful, the real focus of this Joule Agent is something much more important: maintaining incident quality and proper categorization for operational reporting purposes.

The Main Idea Behind the Agent

Traditional incident creation processes often rely heavily on users manually selecting categories, configuration items, and custom classification fields. This leads to inconsistent reporting, incorrect categorization, and operational inefficiencies.

The ServiceNow ITSM Expert agent solves this problem by intelligently determining the correct values using:

The incident description and business context provided by the user
ITSM best practices
Historical incident analysis
Real-time ServiceNow data retrieval
AI reasoning powered by SAP Joule

Instead of assigning random or hardcoded values, the agent dynamically fetches and validates field values directly from ServiceNow before creating or updating incidents.

This logic is accurately handled through the MCP server running on SAP BTP, which acts as the intelligent middleware layer between SAP Joule and ServiceNow.

Intelligent Incident Categorization

The agent does much more than simply filling standard ServiceNow fields. It intelligently determines:

Category, Subcategory , Configuration Item (CMDB_CI), Priority,  Assignment details and  Multiple custom ITSM-specific fields

This becomes especially important when the Configuration Item points to either: SAP applications, Non-SAP systems like Microsoft Dynamics AX, based entirely on the natural language description entered by the user.

What the agent has done here is mostly self-explanatory, but for the keen-eyed readers, here the agent has taken priority 2 as medium, which is not correct (despite all the instructions and testing), but the agent can fix that easily.

I am sure SAP Joule felt embarrassed for this mistake and is admitting to it below (Pun intended) but it fixed its minor oversight and all the fields are properly and accurately filled. 

The corrected result demonstrates how the agent can self-adjust and accurately populate all required fields.

Handling Non-SAP Applications Intelligently

The agent is not limited to SAP systems, if we try the agent for the non-SAP application (eg Microsoft Dynamics AX), the agent has to figure out additional custom fields, accurately, based on the incident description as well as look at past incidents to figure out what it has to do.

When creating incidents related to  AX, the agent must determine additional custom fields unique to the organization’s ITSM implementation. These fields were critical for operational reporting and downstream support processes.

The agent determines these values by: Understanding the incident description,  Looking at historical incidents, Identifying application-specific patterns and Fetching valid values dynamically from ServiceNow

These three are custom fields very specific to our ITSM implementation in ServiceNow and very important and the Joule custom agent has instructions and MCP server running on SAP BTP has internal logic to handle this efficiently and fast (Cached data from MCP on BTP)

The combination of SAP Joule’s reasoning capabilities and the MCP server’s internal logic allows these fields to be handled both efficiently and accurately.

Initially, the process of dynamically fetching valid field values from ServiceNow created a major performance bottleneck.  The custom ServiceNow tables hosting these values were extremely large, and querying thousands of records during every incident creation led to:   Slow response times, Timeout errors and Poor user experience

The core problem was clear — fetching thousands of records on every agent call was wasteful. Fetching thousands of records repeatedly on every agent request was wasteful and inefficient. The solution was introducing a lightweight caching mechanism within the MCP server running on SAP BTP.

The architecture now used In-memory cache, Time-to-Live (TTL) strategy, Periodic refresh every 24 hour. This meant ServiceNow is queried only once per day instead of during every incident creation request.

The MCP server running on SAP BTP is what transforms this from a simple AI skill into a truly autonomous AI agent.

With this architecture, SAP Joule can now Update any incident field dynamically, add comments after testing, Change incident status
Modify priorities, adjust categorization, Trigger workflow actions, handle custom ITSM fields and Interact with ServiceNow entirely through natural language. This creates significantly more optimization potential compared to traditional hardcoded Joule skills.

Also, the SAP Joule agent is not limited to SAP, as Joule can be embedded into MS copilot, Microsoft teams and integrated to talk to other agents and AI copilot.

Conclusion

What started as a few basic custom Joule Skills has now evolved into a far more intelligent and autonomous AI-driven ITSM solution.

By combining SAP Joule, SAP BTP, MCP architecture, ServiceNow APIs, Intelligent caching and AI-powered reasoning, the ServiceNow ITSM Expert agent can not only automate incident creation and updates, but also significantly improve incident quality, categorization accuracy, and operational reporting standards.

The addition of the MCP layer running on SAP BTP transforms SAP Joule from a simple conversational assistant into a powerful enterprise automation agent capable of handling complex ITSM operations through natural language interactions.

And perhaps the most exciting part is this that this is only the beginning.

As enterprise AI ecosystems continue evolving, integrations between SAP Joule with other non-SAP applications will unlock entirely new ways of managing IT operations at scale. 

Note: Generative AI used to formulate the article format

entry.title: Joule Work | Insights
entry.content:
Joule Work brings together AI assistants that help guide decisions and agents that can take action on behalf of users in a single workspace, designed to simplify how people engage with SAP day to day.

Instead of switching between applications and navigating across systems, users can express what they’re trying to accomplish and let the system coordinate the next steps—surfacing relevant information, triggering workflows, and handling routine tasks behind the scenes.

It addresses a real source of friction. Much of today’s enterprise work still depends on knowing where things live, what system to open, and what process to follow. Moving to a more intent-driven interaction model reduces that burden.

The result is a more direct path from “what needs to happen” to “what actually gets done.” As mentioned in #Sapphire2026 " One place to direct the entire business.

Value proposition

  • Transform work with a proactive, outcome-driven user experience. People express a goal and AI orchestrates actions across systems to get it done.
  • Help people spend less time switching applications​ and connecting workflows with a cohesive experience ​that spans your enterprise systems.
  • Speed up processes with proactive assistance that uses ​the deep business context of Joule Agents and Joule Assistants ​to surface relevant data and recommend actions.
  • Keep teams focused on driving decisions and outcomes​ with adaptive, goal-driven workflows that delegate routine ​work to supervised agents.

Check out this link for more information and you can also request a demo Joule Work 

#SAPBusiness AI #JouleWork #SAP Artificial Intelligence Mobile 

Short video

Regards

Shubham

entry.title: Setting up Document Grounding for Joule with Google Drive
entry.content:
SAP's document grounding service lets you connect Joule to your company's internal documents, so it can answer questions grounded in your actual content rather than relying on general training data.  Since recently Google Drive is available as a data source for Document Grounding, which opens this capability up to organizations running on Google Workspace 🎉 

This post walks through the complete setup enabling Document Grounding for Google Drive: from creating a BTP service instance to configuring a GCP service account, wiring up a BTP destination, and creating an ingestion pipeline that indexes your Google Drive documents. The result is a grounding instance ready to serve vector search queries.

A few things that make Google Drive a practical choice here. There is no agent or connector to install in your Drive environment. Native Google Docs and Google Slides are automatically converted to DOCX and PPTX during ingestion. And if your documents already live in a shared drive or shared folder, you can get grounding running without reorganizing anything.

For the full official documentation, see Set Up Document Grounding on SAP Help Portal.

Note: If you are looking for a setup guide for Microsoft SharePoint instead, I highly recommend visiting the blog from @nageshcaparthy : Joule – Getting Started with Document Grounding - setup guide - 1

What You'll Set Up

Here is what this post covers, in order:

  1. Document Grounding service instance on BTP with Cloud Identity Services authentication
  2. GCP service account with credentials that SAP BTP can use to read your Drive
  3. BTP destination that holds the Google Drive connection configuration
  4. Ingestion pipeline that connects the destination to the grounding service and indexes your documents

The BTP destination holds your GCP credentials. The pipeline references that destination to reach Google Drive. The grounding service processes incoming documents into vector embeddings and stores them for search.

Prerequisites

Before you start, make sure you have:

  • Active Joule instance connected to an IAS tenant in your BTP subaccount (see Establish a Unified Joule Instance)
  • BTP subaccount with document grounding entitlement. Entitlements are added automatically to your global account when you purchase AI Units.
  • GCP Console access with permission to create service accounts and download keys
  • Google Drive folder or shared drive containing the documents you want to index, with permission to share access
  • BTP Cockpit access with permissions to create service instances and destinations in your Joule subaccount
  • curl or an API testing tool (e.g. Bruno) for pipeline creation (there is no BTP Cockpit UI for this step)

 

Step 1: Create the Document Grounding Service Instance

Create the Service Instance

Open the BTP Cockpit and navigate to your Joule subaccount. Go to Services > Service Marketplace and search for "Document Grounding". Select the tile and choose Create. Set the Runtime Environment (in this case I chose Other), give the instance a name (you will need this name in the next sub-section), and confirm.

Once the instance is created, open it and create a service binding:

 

Give the binding a name such as "docgrounding-binding" and confirm. When the binding is created, open its details and copy the url value. This is the base URL for all document grounding API calls. It could look like this:

https://mtls.rage.<id>.kyma.ondemand.com

 

Set Up Authentication

The grounding service API uses bearer token authentication. To get tokens, you create a Cloud Identity Services instance linked to your document grounding instance.

Back in Service Marketplace, search for "Cloud Identity Services" and create a new instance. Set the plan to application, the Runtime Environment (in this case I chose Other), and enter a name.

On the parameters screen, provide the following JSON, substituting your document grounding instance name:

{
  "consumed-services": [
    { "service-instance-name": "<your-doc-grounding-instance-name>" }
  ]
}

This is how it looks like:

 

Once this instance exists, navigate to it via Instances and Subscriptions and create a service binding for it with these parameters:

{
  "credential-type": "X509_GENERATED",
  "validity": 365,
  "validity-type": "DAYS"
}

This generates an X.509 certificate that expires after a year. Open the service binding details and copy these four values: clientid, certificate, key, and authorization_endpoint.

 

Get a Bearer Token

Create two files from the certificate and key values. The JSON stores them with literal \n characters rather than real line breaks, so you need to convert them.

On macOS: Open e.g. a Text Editor, Visual Studio Code or another text tool. Copy this bash command in it:

echo '<certificate-value>' | sed 's/\\n/\n/g' > doc-grounding.crt

Get the copied certificate value from the service binding and add it in the bash command. Open a terminal and provide the full bash command, press enter. This should create a doc-grounding.crt file in the current folder you are in.

Repeat the same step again but use this time the following bash command together with the key value from the service binding.

echo '<key-value>' | sed 's/\\n/\n/g' > doc-grounding.key

On Windows PowerShell: Follow the same instructions as for macOS above with the below PowerShell commands.

[System.IO.File]::WriteAllText("doc-grounding.crt", '<certificate-value>'.Replace('\n', "`n"))
[System.IO.File]::WriteAllText("doc-grounding.key", '<key-value>'.Replace('\n', "`n"))

Edit the authorization_endpoint value from the service binding by replacing /oauth2/authorize at the end with /oauth2/token, like for example https://<ias-tenant-id>.accounts.ondemand.com/oauth2/token. Enter the clientid value from the service binding. Then request a token using curl or API tools like Bruno. Here is a sample curl request:

curl \
  --request POST \
  --url "<edited-authorization-endpoint>" \
  --header "accept: application/json" \
  --header "content-type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
  --data "client_id=<clientid>" \
  --data "grant_type=client_credentials" \
  --cert doc-grounding.crt \
  --key doc-grounding.key

For the sake of user experience, let's see how it looks like in Bruno:

Start by creating a new collection (e.g. grounding-blog-post) and adding a POST request named "Get Token". Open the collection settings via the ... menu next to the collection name.

Go to the Client Certificates tab in the collection settings. Now we have to add the certificate and the key twice: Once for the IAS tenant domain in order to get the bearer token, and a second time with the document grounding url as domain with the document grounding url to interact with the document grounding service.

Starting with the first client certificate, as Domain the IAS tenant will be used (e.g. https://<ias-tenant-id>.accounts.ondemand.com). Then, set the Cert file to doc-grounding.crt and the Key file to doc-grounding.key. Leave Passphrase empty. Click Add, then Save.

Afterwards, repeat the steps and only change the domain. You can use the authorization_endpoint base url (or url field from document grounding service binding).

Once saved, the configured certificates appear in the list. Repeat the same for the mtls url which is the service-binding-url.

In the request, set the URL to your authorization_endpoint with /oauth2/token at the end. Open the Body tab, select Form URL Encoded, and add the two fields: client_id with your client ID value, and grant_type set to client_credentials.

 

Send the request. A 200 OK response with an access_token in the body confirms that authentication is working.

 

Copy the access_token value from the response. You will use this for all subsequent API calls, including pipeline creation in Step 4.

 

Verify the Connection

Test that your service URL and token work together. In Bruno, send a GET request to <service-binding-url>/pipeline/api/v1/pipeline with an Accept: application/json header and your bearer token as the Authorization header.

An empty array [] means the connection works and you have no pipelines yet. That is the expected response at this point.

 

Step 2: Create a GCP Service Account

The document grounding service needs machine-to-machine access to Google Drive. A service account gives it that without requiring a human user to go through an OAuth consent screen. You share specific Drive content with the service account, and the BTP destination holds the credentials.

Create a GCP Project

If you do not have a GCP project yet, open the GCP Console and create one. Give it a descriptive name like docgrounding-btp-gdrive so its purpose is clear. Note the Project ID — you cannot change it later.

Enable the Google Drive API

Before creating a service account, make sure the Google Drive API is enabled in your GCP project. Navigate to APIs & Services in the GCP Console and click Enable APIs and services.

 

Search for "Google Drive" and select Google Drive API from the results.

Enable it if it is not already active. Without this, service account credentials will not be able to access Drive content even if the sharing is configured correctly.

 

Create the Service Account

Open the GCP Console and navigate to IAM & Admin > Service Accounts. Create a new service account. The name is your choice; something like btp-doc-grounding makes the purpose clear. You do not need to assign any GCP IAM roles to this service account. Access to specific Drive content is granted through Drive sharing, not through IAM roles.

 

Generate a Key

Select your service account, go to the Keys tab, and choose Add Key > Create new key. Select JSON format and download the file. Keep it secure. It contains the private key that BTP will use to authenticate with Google's OAuth token endpoint.

The JSON structure looks like this:

{
  "type": "service_account",
  "project_id": "<your-gcp-project>",
  "private_key_id": "abc123",
  "private_key": "-----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY-----\n...\n-----END PRIVATE KEY-----\n",
  "client_email": "btp-doc-grounding@<your-project>.iam.gserviceaccount.com",
  "client_id": "123456789012342278901",
  "auth_uri": "https://accounts.google.com/o/oauth2/auth",
  "token_uri": "https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token",
  "auth_provider_x509_cert_url": "https://www.googleapis.com/oauth2/v1/certs",
  "client_x509_cert_url": "https://www.googleapis.com/robot/v1/metadata/x509/btp-doc-grounding@<your-project>.iam.gserviceaccount.com",
  "universe_domain": "googleapis.com"
}

The fields you will use in the next step:

 

JSON FieldWhere You Will Use It
client_idBTP destination Client ID field
private_keyBTP destination additional property private_key
client_emailBTP destination additional property client_email
auth_uriBTP destination additional property auth_uri

 

Share Your Drive Content with the Service Account

Navigate to the Google Drive folder or shared drive you want to index. For this example we go with a shared folder. Use Share and add the client_email value from the JSON as a member. Set the permission to Viewer. Read access is all the ingestion pipeline needs.

Once shared, you can see the folder with the documents you plan to index.

Here is a sample document in that folder that will be indexed through this pipeline:

While you are in Drive, note the folder ID from the URL. For a shared folder, the URL looks like:

https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1ABCdefGHIjklMNOpqrSTUvwxYZ
                                       └── this is your folder ID

For a shared drive, open it and grab the ID from the URL after /drive/. You will need this ID when creating the pipeline in Step 4.

 

Step 3: Configure the BTP Destination

The BTP destination holds your Google Drive connection configuration. The pipeline will reference this destination by name, so the grounding service knows how to authenticate with Google's API.

Open Connectivity > Destinations in your BTP Cockpit and create a new destination with the following main properties:

 

PropertyValue
NameYour choice (e.g. GoogleDrive_DocGrounding)
TypeHTTP
URLhttps://googleapis.com/drive/v3
Proxy TypeInternet
AuthenticationOAuth2ClientCredentials
Client ID<client_id from service account JSON>
Client SecretLeave empty
Token Service URLhttps://oauth2.googleapis.com/token

Then add these additional properties:

 

PropertyValue
private_keyThe full private_key value from the JSON, including the header and footer lines
client_emailThe client_email from the JSON
auth_urihttps://accounts.google.com/o/oauth2/auth

Save the destination and use Check Connection to verify. A successful response confirms that BTP can reach the Google Drive API using the service account credentials.

 

Step 4: Create the Ingestion Pipeline

The pipeline tells the grounding service what to index. It connects your BTP destination to a specific Google Drive folder or shared drive and runs the ingestion process. Pipeline creation happens through the grounding service API, not through the BTP Cockpit.

Here is the curl request for a shared folder:

curl \
  --request POST \
  --url "<service-binding-url>/pipeline/api/v1/pipeline" \
  --header "Authorization: Bearer <access_token>" \
  --header "content-type: application/json" \
  --cert doc-grounding.crt \
  --key doc-grounding.key \
  --data '{
    "type": "GoogleDrive",
    "configuration": {
      "destination": "<your-destination-name>",
      "googleDrive": {
        "resourceType": "SHARED_FOLDER",
        "resourceId": "<your-folder-id>",
        "includePaths": ["/**"]
      }
    }
  }'

The response is a JSON object with a pipelineId. Save this ID. You will use it to check ingestion status.

Here is how the request and response look in Bruno (You can copy the data JSON from the curl request above):

Here is what each field does:

  • type: Must be "GoogleDrive" (case-sensitive).
  • destination: The name of the BTP destination you created in Step 3.
  • resourceType: Either SHARED_FOLDER or SHARED_DRIVE. Use the one that matches what you shared with the service account.
  • resourceId: The folder or shared drive ID from the Drive URL.
  • includePaths: Glob patterns for which files to index. ["/**"] indexes everything, but as this parameter is optional, this can be omitted as well. Use ["/policies/**"] to limit to a specific subfolder.

 

SHARED_FOLDER vs SHARED_DRIVE

 

Resource TypeWhen to Use
SHARED_FOLDERYou shared a specific folder within My Drive or a shared drive
SHARED_DRIVEYou shared an entire shared drive

If you leave resourceId empty, the pipeline runs in auto-discovery mode and indexes all shared drives and folders the service account can read. This is convenient for testing but less controlled for production use.

 

Supported File Types

The grounding service ingests these formats from Google Drive:

DOCX, HTML, JPEG, JPG, JSON, PDF, PNG, PPTX, TIFF, TXT.

Despite that, Document Grounding using Google Drive extends the existing support for DOCX and PPTX with processing of native Google Docs and Google Slides.

For more information and availability, see SAP Document Grounding setup documentation.

 

Verify the Setup

Check Pipeline Status

Use the pipeline ID from the creation response to check ingestion progress:

curl \
  --request GET \
  --url "<service-binding-url>/pipeline/api/v1/pipeline/<pipeline-id>" \
  --header "Authorization: Bearer <access_token>" \
  --cert doc-grounding.crt \
  --key doc-grounding.key

The response includes the pipeline status and a document count. Ingestion for small document sets typically completes within a few minutes. Larger sets take longer.

When ingestion is complete, the status field shows FINISHED.

You can also list the indexed documents by calling <service-binding-url>/pipeline/api/v1/pipeline/<pipeline-id>/executions/<execution-id>/documents. Each indexed document appears with its title, status INDEXED, and a link back to the original Google Drive file.

 

Note: You first have to get the execution id via this request url:

<service-binding-url>/pipeline/api/v1/pipeline/<pipeline-id>/executions

 

Test a Search Query

Now open your Joule Instance that is connected to your IAS. This can be done via Instances and Subscriptions in the BTP Subaccount, then open the Joule instance. Append to your url /webclient/standalone/sap_digital_assistant to open the standalone Joule web client. The full url looks something like that:

https://<tenant>.<region>.sapdas.cloud.sap/webclient/standalone/sap_digital_assistant.

Then provide a prompt that is matching your uploaded document(s).

And here is Joule answering a question about that document's content, with the source citation pointing back to the original file:

And with that, your Google Drive documents are now indexed and searchable through the document grounding service!

Related resources:

  • SAP Help: Set Up Document Grounding
  • SAP Help: Configure Access from SAP BTP
  • SAP Help: Set Up Content Ingestion

entry.title: Autonomous Enterprise beginnt im Betrieb der Software
entry.content:
Die Adoption neuer Technologien und echte Business Transformation setzen eines voraus: einen Software-Betrieb, der die Veränderungen trägt. Denn KI-Szenarien, neue Prozesse und Innovationen entfalten nur dann Wirkung, wenn das operative Fundament stabil, transparent und automatisiert ist.

Warum das gerade jetzt relevant ist

Die KI-Fähigkeiten in SAP-Landschaften wachsen rasant - von Joule als KI-Copilot über intelligente Automatisierung in Geschäftsprozessen bis hin zu zunehmend autonomen, KI-gestützten Funktionen in SAP-Lösungen.

Doch KI anzuwenden heißt nicht, sie einfach „einzuschalten“. Es erfordert Setup, Konfiguration, Anbindung, Monitoring und operative Governance.

Kurz gesagt: SAP Cloud Application Services  unterstützen Unternehmen dabei, KI-Funktionen nicht nur einzuführen, sondern zuverlässig zu operationalisieren - je nach vereinbartem Serviceumfang.

Was SAP Cloud Application Services konkret ermöglichen

  • Fokus auf Geschäftsergebnisse: SAP unterstützt operative Aufgaben rund um Administration, Monitoring und Governance von KI-Funktionen.
  • Stabiler Betrieb: Unterstützung für einen sicheren und skalierbaren Betrieb von KI-Funktionen.
  • Schnellere Adoption: Joule und intelligente Services lassen sich schneller nutzbar machen - mit kürzerer Time-to-Value.
  • Weniger manuelle Fehler: KI-gestützte Ausführung und Insights schaffen mehr Transparenz.
  • Kosteneffizienz: Zugriff auf SAPs globalen Expertenpool statt Aufbau eigener KI-Ops-Teams.
  • Security & Datenschutz: Unterstützung bei Datenschutz- und Sicherheitsanforderungen in KI-Szenarien.

KI liefert nur dann Mehrwert, wenn sie operationalisiert ist. SAP Cloud Application Services schaffen dafür die Grundlage.

Praxisbeispiel: SMA Solar Technology

Wie das in der Praxis aussieht, zeigen Dr. Arndt-Alexander Boehnert, Global Head & VP Cloud Application Services Sales, und Gerhild Muschet, Senior Solution Advisor, in einem Live-Interview am 23. Juni - unter anderem am Beispiel von SMA Solar Technology.

„Der Einsatz privater und öffentlicher Cloud-Lösungen von SAP sowie SAP Cloud Application Services ermöglicht es SMA Solar, sich auf die Weiterentwicklung des Kerngeschäfts und die digitale Transformation zu konzentrieren.

Durch die Entlastung unseres Teams von Routineaufgaben und den Zugang zu SAP-Experten können wir Innovationen schneller umsetzen und flexibel auf Marktveränderungen reagieren. Eine vertrauensvolle Partnerschaft und klare Governance-Strukturen sind entscheidende Erfolgsfaktoren.“

Heiko Kehm
Head of Digital Supply Chain, SMA Solar Technology AG

In der Session erfahrt Ihr

  • Wie ihr ein belastbares Betriebsfundament für KI-Szenarien schafft
  • Welche Pain Points Unternehmen heute erleben - und wie SAP Cloud Application Services über klassische AMS-Modelle hinaus unterstützen
  • Wie der Weg von Discovery bis zum produktiven Start aussehen kann, inklusive Live-Demo und Q&A mit den Experten

Wann? Am 23. Juni 2026, 10:00-10:30 Uhr. Wer Interesse hat, kann sich hier anmelden. Wir freuen uns auf Euch!

entry.title: SAP Business AI Platform: #2 What Exactly It is and Its Components
entry.content:
In the previous blog, we explored the vision of the Autonomous Enterprise - A world where people set the direction…and AI executes.

The natural questions come next are:

  • So..what exactly is SAP Business AI Platform?
  • What are its core components?
  • Where do the AI models get business context?
  • How do AI agents access trusted data?
  • How are security, governance, and compliance maintained?

Let’s dive deep into these!

 

Disclaimer: This blog reflects my personal understanding, interpretation, and point of view. It is not an official publication or statement from SAP.

 

So… What Exactly is SAP Business AI Platform?

At SAP Sapphire 2026, SAP introduced SAP Business AI Platform as the foundation that brings together data, AI, and enterprise grade governance into one unified platform.

 

Let’s understand what it is and why its needed.

Today, customers are not looking for generic AI. They want AI that understands business intent, uses real business context and trusted data. They want AI that knows how business processes work and can take actions across applications. They also want enterprise grade governance and security. 

That means organizations need more than just AI models.

They need a platform that helps them:

  • Build and customize AI solutions
    • Create and orchestrate AI agents
    • Connect data, applications, and processes
    • Govern everything securely and responsibly
    • Scale AI across the entire enterprise

That platform is…SAP Business AI Platform.

In simple words…

SAP Business AI Platform is a unified platform that enables customers, partners, and SAP developers to build, extend, integrate, and govern AI assistants and AI agents across SAP and non-SAP landscapes.

If Autonomous Enterprise is the vision…

SAP Business AI Platform is the foundation that makes it possible.

 

SAP Business AI Platform is NOT a New Solution

SAP Business AI Platform is NOT a new solution but a comprehensive platform of existing solutions and platforms.

It brings together the capabilities of SAP BTP, AI Foundation, SAP BDC, SAP HANA Cloud, and some capabilities from the SAP BTM portfolio.

 

3 core components of SAP Business AI Platform

Build

One environment for building, integrating and running AI agents, applications, agent extensions, and workflows.

Two important components under Build are – Joule Studio and SAP Integration Suite.

Joule Studio

Joule Studio is the next generation of SAP Build offering and is SAP’s AI first development environment for building, and running agents, applications, and workflows.

  • It offers the most productive way to build and extend SAP AI solutions - from intent to requirements to deployment.
  • Joule Studio provides the flexibility of low/no-code and pro-code paths and embeds n8n for visual workflow composition.
  • With the Vercel integration developers can easily build Next.js based web applications using frameworks like React, and Angular complimenting SAP frameworks (UI5, Fiori).

 

SAP Integration Suite

SAP Integration Suite provides a trusted enterprise integration layer that connects AI agents, business processes, systems and, applications across SAP & non-SAP landscapes.

It also facilitates secure governance of enterprise API exposure, multi-LLM connectivity, and enterprise-wide agent executions.

SAP Integration Suite also enables agent connectivity with standard interaction with protocols like MCP and activates event-driven agentic workflows across enterprise systems and processes.

 

Contextualize & Reason

Generic AI can answer questions. But business AI needs to do much more.

SAP grounds AI in more than 50 years of encoded business process expertise. The combination of SAP Knowledge Graph and SAP’s own models is the key differentiator to go beyond the generic LLMs.

At the heart of this capability is Generative AI Hub, which provides access to SAP Domain Models alongside third-party LLMs, with a full suite of orchestration services — including prompt optimizer, benchmarking, evaluation, and data masking.

 

Few important components in this category are: 

 

SAP Knowledge Graph

Data by itself is just information. But businesses run on relationships.

A customer places an order. An order creates a delivery. A delivery triggers an invoice. An invoice impacts finance.

In other words, the relationship is clear:

Customer → Sales Order → Delivery → Invoice → Finance Posting

SAP Knowledge Graph connects all of these business objects and their relationships into a semantic network of meaning.

So… when an AI agent looks at an invoice, it can trace it back to the delivery, the original order, and the customer who initiated the transaction.

In simple words…

SAP Knowledge Graph helps AI understand how everything in your business is connected.

 

SAP Business Data Cloud

AI is only as good as the data it uses. If the data is incomplete, outdated, or inconsistent…the AI results will be unreliable. That is where SAP Business Data Cloud comes in.

 

SAP Business Data Cloud brings the data into a converged Data & Analytics solution spanning data warehousing, master data management, database, analytics, planning, and machine learning.

In simple words…

SAP Business Data Cloud gives AI access to complete, clean, contextual, and current business data.

 

SAP & Non-SAP Models

SAP Business AI Platform does not rely only on third party models from companies such as OpenAI, Anthropic, and Mistral AI.

SAP also provides its own business focused AI models. These models are trained using SAP’s deep understanding of business processes and industry knowledge.

SAP RPT-1 is designed specifically for structured business data. SAP-ABAP-1 is model is built specifically for ABAP development.

 

Govern

Building AI is only half the story.

The bigger questions are:

  • Can we trust it?
  • Is it secure and compliant?
  • Is it actually delivering business value?

SAP Business AI Platform is designed to address both challenges from day one.

Every agent, application, LLM, and MCP server operates within the boundaries the business defines from the moment it goes live.

Every AI initiative is continuously measured against the outcomes it was deployed to deliver.

 

In simple words…

You do not just deploy AI and hope for the best.

You define the rules. You monitor the outcomes. And you continuously verify that AI is creating real business value.

 

How are SAP Business AI Platform and SAP BTP related?

SAP Business AI Platform is SAP’s new comprehensive platform to build, extend, integrate, and govern AI assistants and AI agents across SAP and non-SAP landscapes.

SAP BTP capabilities are a core part of Business AI Platform.

SAP is now positioning SAP Business AI Platform as SAP’s platform, and position BTP’s capabilities as part of this platform.
